variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.PiezoSpeakerV2
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.set_beep
documentation:
Beeps with the given frequency and volume for the duration.

A duration of 0 stops the current beep if any is ongoing.
A duration of 4294967295 results in an infinite beep.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.get_beep
documentation:
Returns the last beep settings as set by {func}`Set Beep`. If a beep is currently
running it also returns the remaining duration of the beep.

If the frequency or volume is updated during a beep (with {func}`Update Frequency`
or {func}`Update Volume`) this function returns the updated value.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.set_alarm
documentation:
Creates an alarm (a tone that goes back and force between two specified frequencies).

The following parameters can be set:

* Start Frequency: Start frequency of the alarm.
* End Frequency: End frequency of the alarm.
* Step Size: Size of one step of the sweep between the start/end frequencies.
* Step Delay: Delay between two steps (duration of time that one tone is used in a sweep).
* Duration: Duration of the alarm.

A duration of 0 stops the current alarm if any is ongoing.
A duration of 4294967295 results in an infinite alarm.

Below you can find two sets of example settings that you can try out. You can use
these as a starting point to find an alarm signal that suits your application.

Example 1: 10 seconds of loud annoying fast alarm

* Start Frequency = 800
* End Frequency = 2000
* Step Size = 10
* Step Delay = 1
* Volume = 10
* Duration = 10000

Example 2: 10 seconds of soft siren sound with slow build-up

* Start Frequency = 250
* End Frequency = 750
* Step Size = 1
* Step Delay = 5
* Volume = 0
* Duration = 10000

The following conditions must be met:

* Start Frequency: has to be smaller than end frequency
* End Frequency: has to be bigger than start frequency
* Step Size: has to be small enough to fit into the frequency range
* Step Delay: has to be small enough to fit into the duration

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.get_alarm
documentation:
Returns the last alarm settings as set by {func}`Set Alarm`. If an alarm is currently
running it also returns the remaining duration of the alarm as well as the
current frequency of the alarm.

If the volume is updated during an alarm (with {func}`Update Volume`)
this function returns the updated value.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.update_volume
documentation: Updates the volume of an ongoing beep or alarm.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.update_frequency
documentation: Updates the frequency of an ongoing beep.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

class_variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_speaker_v2.BrickletPiezoSpeakerV2.DEVICE_URL_PART
documentation: internal
Creates beep and alarm with configurable volume and frequency

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.get_weight
documentation:
Returns the currently measured weight.

If you want to get the weight periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Weight` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Weight Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.set_weight_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Weight` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Weight` callback is only triggered if the weight has changed since the
last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.get_weight_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Weight Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.set_weight_callback_threshold
documentation:
Sets the thresholds for the {cb}`Weight Reached` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Callback is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Callback is triggered when the weight is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Callback is triggered when the weight is *inside* the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Callback is triggered when the weight is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Callback is triggered when the weight is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.get_weight_callback_threshold
documentation: Returns the threshold as set by {func}`Set Weight Callback Threshold`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.set_debounce_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the threshold callback

- {cb}`Weight Reached`

is triggered, if the threshold

- {func}`Set Weight Callback Threshold`

keeps being reached.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.get_debounce_period
documentation: Returns the debounce period as set by {func}`Set Debounce Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.set_moving_average
documentation:
Sets the length of a `moving averaging <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moving_average>`__
for the weight value.

Setting the length to 1 will turn the averaging off. With less
averaging, there is more noise on the data.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.get_moving_average
documentation: Returns the length moving average as set by {func}`Set Moving Average`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.led_on
documentation: Turns the LED on.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.led_off
documentation: Turns the LED off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.is_led_on
documentation: Returns *true* if the led is on, *false* otherwise.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.calibrate
documentation:
To calibrate your Load Cell Bricklet you have to

* empty the scale and call this function with 0 and
* add a known weight to the scale and call this function with the weight.

The calibration is saved in the EEPROM of the Bricklet and only
needs to be done once.

We recommend to use the Brick Viewer for calibration, you don't need
to call this function in your source code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.tare
documentation: Sets the currently measured weight as tare weight.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.set_configuration
documentation:
The measurement rate and gain are configurable.

The rate can be either 10Hz or 80Hz. A faster rate will produce more noise.
It is additionally possible to add a moving average
(see {func}`Set Moving Average`) to the measurements.

The gain can be 128x, 64x or 32x. It represents a measurement range of
±20mV, ±40mV and ±80mV respectively. The Load Cell Bricklet uses an
excitation voltage of 5V and most load cells use an output of 2mV/V. That
means the voltage range is ±15mV for most load cells (i.e. gain of 128x
is best). If you don't know what all of this means you should keep it at
128x, it will most likely be correct.

The configuration is saved in the EEPROM of the Bricklet and only
needs to be done once.

We recommend to use the Brick Viewer for configuration, you don't need
to call this function in your source code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.get_configuration
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

class_variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_load_cell.BrickletLoadCell.DEVICE_URL_PART
documentation: internal
Measures weight with a load cell

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.get_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature of the thermocouple.

If you want to get the temperature periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Temperature` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Temperature Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.set_temperature_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Temperature` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Temperature` callback is only triggered if the temperature has changed
since the last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.get_temperature_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Temperature Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.set_temperature_callback_threshold
documentation:
Sets the thresholds for the {cb}`Temperature Reached` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Callback is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Callback is triggered when the temperature is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Callback is triggered when the temperature is *inside* the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Callback is triggered when the temperature is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Callback is triggered when the temperature is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.get_temperature_callback_threshold
documentation: Returns the threshold as set by {func}`Set Temperature Callback Threshold`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.set_debounce_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the threshold callback

- {cb}`Temperature Reached`

is triggered, if the threshold

- {func}`Set Temperature Callback Threshold`

keeps being reached.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.get_debounce_period
documentation: Returns the debounce period as set by {func}`Set Debounce Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.set_configuration
documentation:
You can configure averaging size, thermocouple type and frequency
filtering.

Available averaging sizes are 1, 2, 4, 8 and 16 samples.

As thermocouple type you can use B, E, J, K, N, R, S and T. If you have a
different thermocouple or a custom thermocouple you can also use
G8 and G32. With these types the returned value will not be in °C/100,
it will be calculated by the following formulas:

- G8: `value = 8 * 1.6 * 2^17 * Vin`
- G32: `value = 32 * 1.6 * 2^17 * Vin`

where Vin is the thermocouple input voltage.

The frequency filter can be either configured to 50Hz or to 60Hz. You should
configure it according to your utility frequency.

The conversion time depends on the averaging and filter configuration, it can
be calculated as follows:

- 60Hz: `time = 82 + (samples - 1) * 16.67`
- 50Hz: `time = 98 + (samples - 1) * 20`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.get_configuration
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.get_error_state
documentation:
Returns the current error state. There are two possible errors:

- Over/Under Voltage and
- Open Circuit.

Over/Under Voltage happens for voltages below 0V or above 3.3V. In this case
it is very likely that your thermocouple is defective. An Open Circuit error
indicates that there is no thermocouple connected.

You can use the {cb}`Error State` callback to automatically get triggered
when the error state changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_thermocouple.BrickletThermocouple.get_identity
documentation:

the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

variables.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.Servo
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.enable
documentation:
Enables a servo (0 to 6). If a servo is enabled, the configured position,
velocity, acceleration, etc. are applied immediately.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.disable
documentation:
Disables a servo (0 to 6). Disabled servos are not driven at all, i.e. a
disabled servo will not hold its position if a load is applied.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.is_enabled
documentation: Returns *true* if the specified servo is enabled, *false* otherwise.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_position
documentation:
Sets the position for the specified servo.

The default range of the position is -9000 to 9000, but it can be specified
according to your servo with {func}`Set Degree`.

If you want to control a linear servo or RC brushless motor controller or
similar with the Servo Brick, you can also define lengths or speeds with
{func}`Set Degree`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_position
documentation: Returns the position of the specified servo as set by {func}`Set Position`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_current_position
documentation:
Returns the *current* position of the specified servo. This may not be the
value of {func}`Set Position` if the servo is currently approaching a
position goal.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_velocity
documentation:
Sets the maximum velocity of the specified servo. The velocity
is accelerated according to the value set by {func}`Set Acceleration`.

The minimum velocity is 0 (no movement) and the maximum velocity is 65535.
With a value of 65535 the position will be set immediately (no velocity).

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_velocity
documentation: Returns the velocity of the specified servo as set by {func}`Set Velocity`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_current_velocity
documentation:
Returns the *current* velocity of the specified servo. This may not be the
value of {func}`Set Velocity` if the servo is currently approaching a
velocity goal.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_acceleration
documentation:
Sets the acceleration of the specified servo.

The minimum acceleration is 1 and the maximum acceleration is 65535.
With a value of 65535 the velocity will be set immediately (no acceleration).

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_acceleration
documentation:
Returns the acceleration for the specified servo as set by
{func}`Set Acceleration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_output_voltage
documentation:
Sets the output voltages with which the servos are driven.

:::{note}
We recommend that you set this value to the maximum voltage that is
specified for your servo, most servos achieve their maximum force only
with high voltages.
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_output_voltage
documentation: Returns the output voltage as specified by {func}`Set Output Voltage`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_pulse_width
documentation:
Sets the minimum and maximum pulse width of the specified servo.

Usually, servos are controlled with a
`PWM <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pulse-width_modulation>`__, whereby the
length of the pulse controls the position of the servo. Every servo has
different minimum and maximum pulse widths, these can be specified with
this function.

If you have a datasheet for your servo that specifies the minimum and
maximum pulse width, you should set the values accordingly. If your servo
comes without any datasheet you have to find the values via trial and error.

The minimum must be smaller than the maximum.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_pulse_width
documentation:
Returns the minimum and maximum pulse width for the specified servo as set by
{func}`Set Pulse Width`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_degree
documentation:
Sets the minimum and maximum degree for the specified servo (by default
given as °/100).

This only specifies the abstract values between which the minimum and maximum
pulse width is scaled. For example: If you specify a pulse width of 1000µs
to 2000µs and a degree range of -90° to 90°, a call of {func}`Set Position`
with 0 will result in a pulse width of 1500µs
(-90° = 1000µs, 90° = 2000µs, etc.).

Possible usage:

- The datasheet of your servo specifies a range of 200° with the middle position
  at 110°. In this case you can set the minimum to -9000 and the maximum to 11000.
- You measure a range of 220° on your servo and you don't have or need a middle
  position. In this case you can set the minimum to 0 and the maximum to 22000.
- You have a linear servo with a drive length of 20cm, In this case you could
  set the minimum to 0 and the maximum to 20000. Now you can set the Position
  with {func}`Set Position` with a resolution of cm/100. Also the velocity will
  have a resolution of cm/100s and the acceleration will have a resolution of
  cm/100s².
- You don't care about units and just want the highest possible resolution. In
  this case you should set the minimum to -32767 and the maximum to 32767.
- You have a brushless motor with a maximum speed of 10000 rpm and want to
  control it with a RC brushless motor controller. In this case you can set the
  minimum to 0 and the maximum to 10000. {func}`Set Position` now controls the rpm.

The minimum must be smaller than the maximum.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_degree
documentation:
Returns the minimum and maximum degree for the specified servo as set by
{func}`Set Degree`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_period
documentation:
Sets the period of the specified servo.

Usually, servos are controlled with a
`PWM <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pulse-width_modulation>`__. Different
servos expect PWMs with different periods. Most servos run well with a
period of about 20ms.

If your servo comes with a datasheet that specifies a period, you should
set it accordingly. If you don't have a datasheet and you have no idea
what the correct period is, the default value will most likely
work fine.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_period
documentation: Returns the period for the specified servo as set by {func}`Set Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_servo_current
documentation: Returns the current consumption of the specified servo.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_overall_current
documentation: Returns the current consumption of all servos together.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_stack_input_voltage
documentation:
Returns the stack input voltage. The stack input voltage is the
voltage that is supplied via the stack, i.e. it is given by a
Step-Down or Step-Up Power Supply.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_external_input_voltage
documentation:
Returns the external input voltage. The external input voltage is
given via the black power input connector on the Servo Brick.

If there is an external input voltage and a stack input voltage, the motors
will be driven by the external input voltage. If there is only a stack
voltage present, the motors will be driven by this voltage.

:::{warning}
This means, if you have a high stack voltage and a low external voltage,
the motors will be driven with the low external voltage. If you then remove
the external connection, it will immediately be driven by the high
stack voltage
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_minimum_voltage
documentation:
Sets the minimum voltage, below which the {cb}`Under Voltage` callback
is triggered. The minimum possible value that works with the Servo Brick is 5V.
You can use this function to detect the discharge of a battery that is used
to drive the stepper motor. If you have a fixed power supply, you likely do
not need this functionality.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_minimum_voltage
documentation: Returns the minimum voltage as set by {func}`Set Minimum Voltage`

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.enable_position_reached_callback
documentation:
Enables the {cb}`Position Reached` callback.

Default is disabled.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.disable_position_reached_callback
documentation:
Disables the {cb}`Position Reached` callback.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.is_position_reached_callback_enabled
documentation:
Returns *true* if {cb}`Position Reached` callback is enabled, *false* otherwise.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.enable_velocity_reached_callback
documentation:
Enables the {cb}`Velocity Reached` callback.

Default is disabled.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.disable_velocity_reached_callback
documentation:
Disables the {cb}`Velocity Reached` callback.

Default is disabled.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.is_velocity_reached_callback_enabled
documentation:
Returns *true* if {cb}`Velocity Reached` callback is enabled, *false* otherwise.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_spitfp_baudrate_config
documentation:
The SPITF protocol can be used with a dynamic baudrate. If the dynamic baudrate is
enabled, the Brick will try to adapt the baudrate for the communication
between Bricks and Bricklets according to the amount of data that is transferred.

The baudrate will be increased exponentially if lots of data is sent/received and
decreased linearly if little data is sent/received.

This lowers the baudrate in applications where little data is transferred (e.g.
a weather station) and increases the robustness. If there is lots of data to transfer
(e.g. Thermal Imaging Bricklet) it automatically increases the baudrate as needed.

In cases where some data has to transferred as fast as possible every few seconds
(e.g. RS485 Bricklet with a high baudrate but small payload) you may want to turn
the dynamic baudrate off to get the highest possible performance.

The maximum value of the baudrate can be set per port with the function
{func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate`. If the dynamic baudrate is disabled, the baudrate
as set by {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate` will be used statically.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.4$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_spitfp_baudrate_config
documentation:
Returns the baudrate config, see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate Config`.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.4$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_send_timeout_count
documentation:
Returns the timeout count for the different communication methods.

The methods 0-2 are available for all Bricks, 3-7 only for Master Bricks.

This function is mostly used for debugging during development, in normal operation
the counters should nearly always stay at 0.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.2$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.set_spitfp_baudrate
documentation:
Sets the baudrate for a specific Bricklet port.

If you want to increase the throughput of Bricklets you can increase
the baudrate. If you get a high error count because of high
interference (see {func}`Get SPITFP Error Count`) you can decrease the
baudrate.

If the dynamic baudrate feature is enabled, the baudrate set by this
function corresponds to the maximum baudrate (see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate Config`).

Regulatory testing is done with the default baudrate. If CE compatibility
or similar is necessary in your applications we recommend to not change
the baudrate.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.2$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_spitfp_baudrate
documentation:
Returns the baudrate for a given Bricklet port, see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate`.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.2$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

- ACK checksum errors,
- message checksum errors,
- framing errors and
- overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Brick side. All
Bricklets have a similar function that returns the errors on the Bricklet side.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.2$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.enable_status_led
documentation:
Enables the status LED.

The status LED is the blue LED next to the USB connector. If enabled is is
on and it flickers if data is transfered. If disabled it is always off.

The default state is enabled.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.disable_status_led
documentation:
Disables the status LED.

The status LED is the blue LED next to the USB connector. If enabled is is
on and it flickers if data is transfered. If disabled it is always off.

The default state is enabled.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.is_status_led_enabled
documentation:
Returns *true* if the status LED is enabled, *false* otherwise.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_protocol1_bricklet_name
documentation:
Returns the firmware and protocol version and the name of the Bricklet for a
given port.

This functions sole purpose is to allow automatic flashing of v1.x.y Bricklet
plugins.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has an
accuracy of ±15%. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Brick. Calling this function
on a Brick inside of a stack will reset the whole stack.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.write_bricklet_plugin
documentation:
Writes 32 bytes of firmware to the bricklet attached at the given port.
The bytes are written to the position offset * 32.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.read_bricklet_plugin
documentation:
Reads 32 bytes of firmware from the bricklet attached at the given port.
The bytes are read starting at the position offset * 32.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Brick is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position is the position in the stack from '0' (bottom) to '8' (top).

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

class_variables.name: tinkerforge.brick_servo.BrickServo.DEVICE_URL_PART
documentation: internal
Drives up to 7 RC Servos with up to 3A

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.RotaryPotiV2
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.get_position
documentation:
Returns the position of the rotary potentiometer. The value is
between -150° (turned left) and 150° (turned right).

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Position` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Position Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.set_position_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Position` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change
within the period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

It is furthermore possible to constrain the callback with thresholds.

The `option`-parameter together with min/max sets a threshold for the {cb}`Position` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Threshold is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *inside* or equal to the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

If the option is set to 'x' (threshold turned off) the callback is triggered with the fixed period.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.get_position_callback_configuration
documentation: Returns the callback configuration as set by {func}`Set Position Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

class_variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_rotary_poti_v2.BrickletRotaryPotiV2.DEVICE_URL_PART
documentation: internal
300° rotary potentiometer

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.MultiTouchV2
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.get_touch_state
documentation:
Returns the current touch state. The state is given as a array of
bools.

Element 0 to 11 represent the 12 electrodes and element 12 represents
the proximity.

If an electrode is touched, the corresponding element is *true*. If
a hand or similar is in proximity to the electrodes, element 12 is
*true*.

The proximity is activated with a distance of 1-2cm. An electrode
is already counted as touched if a finger is nearly touching the
electrode. This means that you can put a piece of paper or foil
or similar on top of a electrode to build a touch panel with
a professional look.

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Touch State` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Touch State Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.set_touch_state_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Touch State` callback
is triggered periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change within the
period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.get_touch_state_callback_configuration
documentation:
Returns the callback configuration as set by
{func}`Set Touch State Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.recalibrate
documentation:
Recalibrates the electrodes. Call this function whenever you changed
or moved you electrodes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.set_electrode_config
documentation:
Enables/disables electrodes with a bool array (see {func}`Get Touch State`).

*True* enables the electrode, *false* disables the electrode. A
disabled electrode will always return *false* as its state. If you
don't need all electrodes you can disable the electrodes that are
not needed.

It is recommended that you disable the proximity electrode (element 12) if
the proximity feature is not needed. This will reduce the amount of
traffic that is produced by the {cb}`Touch State` callback.

Disabling electrodes will also reduce power consumption.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.get_electrode_config
documentation: Returns the electrode configuration, as set by {func}`Set Electrode Config`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.set_electrode_sensitivity
documentation:
Sets the sensitivity of the electrodes. An electrode with a high sensitivity
will register a touch earlier then an electrode with a low sensitivity.

If you build a big electrode you might need to decrease the sensitivity, since
the area that can be charged will get bigger. If you want to be able to
activate an electrode from further away you need to increase the sensitivity.

After a new sensitivity is set, you likely want to call {func}`Recalibrate`
to calibrate the electrodes with the newly defined sensitivity.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.get_electrode_sensitivity
documentation: Returns the current sensitivity, as set by {func}`Set Electrode Sensitivity`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.set_touch_led_config
documentation:
Configures the touch LED to be either turned off, turned on, blink in
heartbeat mode or show the touch state (electrode touched = LED on).

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.get_touch_led_config
documentation: Returns the LED configuration as set by {func}`Set Touch LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_multi_touch_v2.BrickletMultiTouchV2.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.OLED128x64
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.BrickletOLED128x64

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.BrickletOLED128x64.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.BrickletOLED128x64.write
documentation:
Appends 64 byte of data to the window as set by {func}`New Window`.

Each row has a height of 8 pixels which corresponds to one byte of data.

Example: if you call {func}`New Window` with column from 0 to 127 and row
from 0 to 7 (the whole display) each call of {func}`Write` (red arrow) will
write half of a row.

```{image} /Images/Bricklets/bricklet_oled_128x64_display.png
:align: center
:alt: Display pixel order
:scale: 100 %
:target: ../../_images/Bricklets/bricklet_oled_128x64_display.png
```

The LSB (D0) of each data byte is at the top and the MSB (D7) is at the
bottom of the row.

The next call of {func}`Write` will write the second half of the row
and the next two the second row and so on. To fill the whole display
you need to call {func}`Write` 16 times.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.BrickletOLED128x64.new_window
documentation:
Sets the window in which you can write with {func}`Write`. One row
has a height of 8 pixels.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.BrickletOLED128x64.clear_display
documentation: Clears the current content of the window as set by {func}`New Window`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.BrickletOLED128x64.set_display_configuration
documentation:
Sets the configuration of the display.

You can set a contrast value from 0 to 255 and you can invert the color
(black/white) of the display.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.BrickletOLED128x64.get_display_configuration
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Display Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.BrickletOLED128x64.write_line
documentation:
Writes text to a specific line with a specific position.
The text can have a maximum of 26 characters.

For example: (1, 10, "Hello") will write *Hello* in the middle of the
second line of the display.

You can draw to the display with {func}`Write` and then add text to it
afterwards.

The display uses a special 5x7 pixel charset. You can view the characters
of the charset in Brick Viewer.

The font conforms to code page 437.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64.BrickletOLED128x64.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.PTCV2
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature of the connected sensor.

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Temperature` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Temperature Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.set_temperature_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Temperature` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change
within the period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

It is furthermore possible to constrain the callback with thresholds.

The `option`-parameter together with min/max sets a threshold for the {cb}`Temperature` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Threshold is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *inside* or equal to the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

If the option is set to 'x' (threshold turned off) the callback is triggered with the fixed period.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_temperature_callback_configuration
documentation: Returns the callback configuration as set by {func}`Set Temperature Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_resistance
documentation:
Returns the value as measured by the MAX31865 precision delta-sigma ADC.

The value can be converted with the following formulas:

- Pt100: resistance = (value * 390) / 32768
- Pt1000: resistance = (value * 3900) / 32768

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Resistance` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Resistance Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.set_resistance_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Resistance` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change
within the period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

It is furthermore possible to constrain the callback with thresholds.

The `option`-parameter together with min/max sets a threshold for the {cb}`Resistance` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Threshold is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *inside* or equal to the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

If the option is set to 'x' (threshold turned off) the callback is triggered with the fixed period.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_resistance_callback_configuration
documentation: Returns the callback configuration as set by {func}`Set Resistance Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.set_noise_rejection_filter
documentation:
Sets the noise rejection filter to either 50Hz (0) or 60Hz (1).
Noise from 50Hz or 60Hz power sources (including
harmonics of the AC power's fundamental frequency) is
attenuated by 82dB.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_noise_rejection_filter
documentation:
Returns the noise rejection filter option as set by
{func}`Set Noise Rejection Filter`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.is_sensor_connected
documentation:
Returns *true* if the sensor is connected correctly.

If this function
returns *false*, there is either no Pt100 or Pt1000 sensor connected,
the sensor is connected incorrectly or the sensor itself is faulty.

If you want to get the status automatically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Sensor Connected` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Sensor Connected Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.set_wire_mode
documentation:
Sets the wire mode of the sensor. Possible values are 2, 3 and 4 which
correspond to 2-, 3- and 4-wire sensors. The value has to match the jumper
configuration on the Bricklet.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_wire_mode
documentation: Returns the wire mode as set by {func}`Set Wire Mode`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.set_moving_average_configuration
documentation:
Sets the length of a `moving averaging <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moving_average>`__
for the resistance and temperature.

Setting the length to 1 will turn the averaging off. With less
averaging, there is more noise on the data.

New data is gathered every 20ms. With a moving average of length 1000 the resulting
averaging window has a length of 20s. If you want to do long term measurements the longest
moving average will give the cleanest results.

The default values match the non-changeable averaging settings of the old PTC Bricklet 1.0

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_moving_average_configuration
documentation: Returns the moving average configuration as set by {func}`Set Moving Average Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.set_sensor_connected_callback_configuration
documentation:
If you enable this callback, the {cb}`Sensor Connected` callback is triggered
every time a Pt sensor is connected/disconnected.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_sensor_connected_callback_configuration
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Sensor Connected Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

class_variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_ptc_v2.BrickletPTCV2.DEVICE_URL_PART
documentation: internal
Reads temperatures from Pt100 und Pt1000 sensors

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
This file was automatically generated on 2024-02-27.      #
Python Bindings Version 2.1.31                            #

variables.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.RED
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.release_object
documentation:
Decreases the reference count of an object by one and returns the resulting
error code. If the reference count reaches zero the object gets destroyed.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.allocate_string
documentation:
Allocates a new string object, reserves `length_to_reserve` bytes memory
for it and sets up to the first 60 bytes. Set `length_to_reserve` to the
length of the string that should be stored in the string object.

Returns the object ID of the new string object and the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.truncate_string
documentation:
Truncates a string object to `length` bytes and returns the resulting
error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_string_length
documentation: Returns the length of a string object and the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.set_string_chunk
documentation:
Sets a chunk of up to 58 bytes in a string object beginning at `offset`.

Returns the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_string_chunk
documentation:
Returns a chunk up to 63 bytes from a string object beginning at `offset` and
returns the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.allocate_list
documentation:
Allocates a new list object and reserves memory for `length_to_reserve`
items. Set `length_to_reserve` to the number of items that should be stored
in the list object.

Returns the object ID of the new list object and the resulting error code.

When a list object gets destroyed then the reference count of each object in
the list object is decreased by one.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_list_length
documentation: Returns the length of a list object in items and the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_list_item
documentation:
Returns the object ID and type of the object stored at `index` in a list
object and returns the resulting error code.

Possible object types are:

- String = 0
- List = 1
- File = 2
- Directory = 3
- Process = 4
- Program = 5

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.append_to_list
documentation:
Appends an object to a list object and increases the reference count of the
appended object by one.

Returns the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.remove_from_list
documentation:
Removes the object stored at `index` from a list object and decreases the
reference count of the removed object by one.

Returns the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.open_file
documentation:
Opens an existing file or creates a new file and allocates a new file object
for it.

FIXME: name has to be absolute

The reference count of the name string object is increased by one. When the
file object gets destroyed then the reference count of the name string object is
decreased by one. Also the name string object is locked and cannot be modified
while the file object holds a reference to it.

The `flags` parameter takes a ORed combination of the following possible file
flags (in hexadecimal notation):

- ReadOnly = 0x0001 (O_RDONLY)
- WriteOnly = 0x0002 (O_WRONLY)
- ReadWrite = 0x0004 (O_RDWR)
- Append = 0x0008 (O_APPEND)
- Create = 0x0010 (O_CREAT)
- Exclusive = 0x0020 (O_EXCL)
- NonBlocking = 0x0040 (O_NONBLOCK)
- Truncate = 0x0080 (O_TRUNC)
- Temporary = 0x0100
- Replace = 0x0200

FIXME: explain *Temporary* and *Replace* flag

The `permissions` parameter takes a ORed combination of the following
possible file permissions (in octal notation) that match the common UNIX
permission bits:

- UserRead = 00400
- UserWrite = 00200
- UserExecute = 00100
- GroupRead = 00040
- GroupWrite = 00020
- GroupExecute = 00010
- OthersRead = 00004
- OthersWrite = 00002
- OthersExecute = 00001

Returns the object ID of the new file object and the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.create_pipe
documentation:
Creates a new pipe and allocates a new file object for it.

The `flags` parameter takes a ORed combination of the following possible
pipe flags (in hexadecimal notation):

- NonBlockingRead = 0x0001
- NonBlockingWrite = 0x0002

The length of the pipe buffer can be specified with the `length` parameter
in bytes. If length is set to zero, then the default pipe buffer length is used.

Returns the object ID of the new file object and the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_file_info
documentation:
Returns various information about a file and the resulting error code.

Possible file types are:

- Unknown = 0
- Regular = 1
- Directory = 2
- Character = 3
- Block = 4
- FIFO = 5
- Symlink = 6
- Socket = 7
- Pipe = 8

If the file type is *Pipe* then the returned name string object is invalid,
because a pipe has no name. Otherwise the returned name string object was used
to open or create the file object, as passed to {func}`Open File`.

The returned flags were used to open or create the file object, as passed to
{func}`Open File` or {func}`Create Pipe`. See the respective function for a list
of possible file and pipe flags.

FIXME: everything except flags and length is invalid if file type is *Pipe*

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.read_file
documentation:
Reads up to 62 bytes from a file object.

Returns the bytes read, the actual number of bytes read and the resulting
error code.

If there is not data to be read, either because the file position reached
end-of-file or because there is not data in the pipe, then zero bytes are
returned.

If the file object was created by {func}`Open File` without the *NonBlocking*
flag or by {func}`Create Pipe` without the *NonBlockingRead* flag then the
error code *NotSupported* is returned.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.read_file_async
documentation:
Reads up to 2{sup}`63` - 1 bytes from a file object asynchronously.

Reports the bytes read (in 60 byte chunks), the actual number of bytes read and
the resulting error code via the {cb}`Async File Read` callback.

If there is not data to be read, either because the file position reached
end-of-file or because there is not data in the pipe, then zero bytes are
reported.

If the file object was created by {func}`Open File` without the *NonBlocking*
flag or by {func}`Create Pipe` without the *NonBlockingRead* flag then the error
code *NotSupported* is reported via the {cb}`Async File Read` callback.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.abort_async_file_read
documentation:
Aborts a {func}`Read File Async` operation in progress.

Returns the resulting error code.

On success the {cb}`Async File Read` callback will report *OperationAborted*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.write_file
documentation:
Writes up to 61 bytes to a file object.

Returns the actual number of bytes written and the resulting error code.

If the file object was created by {func}`Open File` without the *NonBlocking*
flag or by {func}`Create Pipe` without the *NonBlockingWrite* flag then the
error code *NotSupported* is returned.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.write_file_unchecked
documentation:
Writes up to 61 bytes to a file object.

Does neither report the actual number of bytes written nor the resulting error
code.

If the file object was created by {func}`Open File` without the *NonBlocking*
flag or by {func}`Create Pipe` without the *NonBlockingWrite* flag then the
write operation will fail silently.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.write_file_async
documentation:
Writes up to 61 bytes to a file object.

Reports the actual number of bytes written and the resulting error code via the
{cb}`Async File Write` callback.

If the file object was created by {func}`Open File` without the *NonBlocking*
flag or by {func}`Create Pipe` without the *NonBlockingWrite* flag then the
error code *NotSupported* is reported via the {cb}`Async File Write` callback.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.set_file_position
documentation:
Set the current seek position of a file object relative to `origin`.

Possible file origins are:

- Beginning = 0
- Current = 1
- End = 2

Returns the resulting absolute seek position and error code.

If the file object was created by {func}`Create Pipe` then it has no seek
position and the error code *InvalidSeek* is returned.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_file_position
documentation:
Returns the current seek position of a file object and returns the
resulting error code.

If the file object was created by {func}`Create Pipe` then it has no seek
position and the error code *InvalidSeek* is returned.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.open_directory
documentation:
Opens an existing directory and allocates a new directory object for it.

FIXME: name has to be absolute

The reference count of the name string object is increased by one. When the
directory object is destroyed then the reference count of the name string
object is decreased by one. Also the name string object is locked and cannot be
modified while the directory object holds a reference to it.

Returns the object ID of the new directory object and the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_directory_name
documentation:
Returns the name of a directory object, as passed to {func}`Open Directory`, and
the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_next_directory_entry
documentation:
Returns the next entry in a directory object and the resulting error code.

If there is not next entry then error code *NoMoreData* is returned. To rewind
a directory object call {func}`Rewind Directory`.

Possible directory entry types are:

- Unknown = 0
- Regular = 1
- Directory = 2
- Character = 3
- Block = 4
- FIFO = 5
- Symlink = 6
- Socket = 7

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.rewind_directory
documentation: Rewinds a directory object and returns the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.create_directory
documentation: FIXME: name has to be absolute

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.kill_process
documentation:
Sends a UNIX signal to a process object and returns the resulting error code.

Possible UNIX signals are:

* Interrupt = 2
* Quit = 3
* Abort = 6
* Kill = 9
* User1 = 10
* User2 = 12
* Terminate = 15
* Continue =  18
* Stop = 19

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_process_command
documentation:
Returns the executable, arguments, environment and working directory used to
spawn a process object, as passed to {func}`Spawn Process`, and the resulting
error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_process_identity
documentation:
Returns the process ID and the user and group ID used to spawn a process object,
as passed to {func}`Spawn Process`, and the resulting error code.

The process ID is only valid if the state is *Running* or *Stopped*, see
{func}`Get Process State`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_process_stdio
documentation:
Returns the stdin, stdout and stderr files used to spawn a process object, as
passed to {func}`Spawn Process`, and the resulting error code.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_red.BrickRED.get_process_state
documentation:
Returns the current state, timestamp and exit code of a process object, and
the resulting error code.

Possible process states are:

* Unknown = 0
* Running = 1
* Error = 2
* Exited = 3
* Killed = 4
* Stopped = 5

The timestamp represents the UNIX time since when the process is in its current
state.

The exit code is only valid if the state is *Error*, *Exited*, *Killed* or
*Stopped* and has different meanings depending on the state:

* Error: error code for error occurred while spawning the process (see below)
* Exited: exit status of the process
* Killed: UNIX signal number used to kill the process
* Stopped: UNIX signal number used to stop the process

Possible exit/error codes in *Error* state are:

* InternalError = 125
* CannotExecute = 126
* DoesNotExist = 127

The *CannotExecute* error can be caused by the executable being opened for
writing.

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_voltage_current_v2.VoltageCurrentV2
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_voltage_current_v2.BrickletVoltageCurrentV2

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_voltage_current_v2.BrickletVoltageCurrentV2.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_voltage_current_v2.BrickletVoltageCurrentV2.get_current
documentation:
Returns the current.

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Current` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Current Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_voltage_current_v2.BrickletVoltageCurrentV2.set_current_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Current` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change
within the period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

It is furthermore possible to constrain the callback with thresholds.

The `option`-parameter together with min/max sets a threshold for the {cb}`Current` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Threshold is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *inside* or equal to the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

If the option is set to 'x' (threshold turned off) the callback is triggered with the fixed period.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_voltage_current_v2.BrickletVoltageCurrentV2.get_current_callback_configuration
documentation: Returns the callback configuration as set by {func}`Set Current Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_voltage_current_v2.BrickletVoltageCurrentV2.get_voltage
documentation:
Returns the voltage.

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Voltage` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Voltage Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_voltage_current_v2.BrickletVoltageCurrentV2.set_voltage_callback_configuration

periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

It is furthermore possible to constrain the callback with thresholds.

The `option`-parameter together with min/max sets a threshold for the {cb}`Voltage` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Threshold is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *inside* or equal to the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

If the option is set to 'x' (threshold turned off) the callback is triggered with the fixed period.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_voltage_current_v2.BrickletVoltageCurrentV2.get_voltage_callback_configuration

documentation:

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Power` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Power Callback Configuration`.

periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
