variables.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.IMU
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_acceleration
documentation:
Returns the calibrated acceleration from the accelerometer for the
x, y and z axis.

If you want to get the acceleration periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Acceleration` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Acceleration Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_magnetic_field
documentation:
Returns the calibrated magnetic field from the magnetometer for the
x, y and z axis.

If you want to get the magnetic field periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Magnetic Field` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Magnetic Field Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_angular_velocity
documentation:
Returns the calibrated angular velocity from the gyroscope for the
x, y and z axis in °/14.375s (you have to divide by 14.375 to
get the value in °/s).

If you want to get the angular velocity periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Angular Velocity` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Angular Velocity Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_all_data
documentation:
Returns the data from {func}`Get Acceleration`, {func}`Get Magnetic Field`
and {func}`Get Angular Velocity` as well as the temperature of the IMU Brick.

If you want to get the data periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`All Data` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set All Data Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_orientation
documentation:
Returns the current orientation (roll, pitch, yaw) of the IMU Brick as Euler
angles. Note that Euler angles always experience a
[gimbal lock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gimbal_lock).

We recommend that you use quaternions instead.

The order to sequence in which the orientation values should be applied is
roll, yaw, pitch.

If you want to get the orientation periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Orientation` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Orientation Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_quaternion
documentation:
Returns the current orientation (x, y, z, w) of the IMU as
[quaternions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quaternions_and_spatial_rotation).

You can go from quaternions to Euler angles with the following formula:

```
xAngle = atan2(2*y*w - 2*x*z, 1 - 2*y*y - 2*z*z)
yAngle = atan2(2*x*w - 2*y*z, 1 - 2*x*x - 2*z*z)
zAngle =  asin(2*x*y + 2*z*w)
```

This process is not reversible, because of the
[gimbal lock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gimbal_lock).

It is also possible to calculate independent angles. You can calculate
yaw, pitch and roll in a right-handed vehicle coordinate system according to
DIN70000 with:

```
yaw   =  atan2(2*x*y + 2*w*z, w*w + x*x - y*y - z*z)
pitch = -asin(2*w*y - 2*x*z)
roll  = -atan2(2*y*z + 2*w*x, -w*w + x*x + y*y - z*z))
```

Converting the quaternions to an OpenGL transformation matrix is
possible with the following formula:

```
matrix = [[1 - 2*(y*y + z*z),     2*(x*y - w*z),     2*(x*z + w*y), 0],
          [    2*(x*y + w*z), 1 - 2*(x*x + z*z),     2*(y*z - w*x), 0],
          [    2*(x*z - w*y),     2*(y*z + w*x), 1 - 2*(x*x + y*y), 0],
          [                0,                 0,                 0, 1]]
```

If you want to get the quaternions periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Quaternion` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Quaternion Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_imu_temperature
documentation: Returns the temperature of the IMU Brick.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.leds_on
documentation: Turns the orientation and direction LEDs of the IMU Brick on.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.leds_off
documentation: Turns the orientation and direction LEDs of the IMU Brick off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.are_leds_on
documentation:
Returns *true* if the orientation and direction LEDs of the IMU Brick
are on, *false* otherwise.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.set_acceleration_range
documentation: Not implemented yet.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_acceleration_range
documentation: Not implemented yet.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.set_magnetometer_range
documentation: Not implemented yet.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_magnetometer_range
documentation: Not implemented yet.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.set_convergence_speed
documentation:
Sets the convergence speed of the IMU Brick. The convergence speed
determines how the different sensor measurements are fused.

If the orientation of the IMU Brick is off by 10° and the convergence speed is
set to 20°/s, it will take 0.5s until the orientation is corrected. However,
if the correct orientation is reached and the convergence speed is too high,
the orientation will fluctuate with the fluctuations of the accelerometer and
the magnetometer.

If you set the convergence speed to 0, practically only the gyroscope is used
to calculate the orientation. This gives very smooth movements, but errors of the
gyroscope will not be corrected. If you set the convergence speed to something
above 500, practically only the magnetometer and the accelerometer are used to
calculate the orientation. In this case the movements are abrupt and the values
will fluctuate, but there won't be any errors that accumulate over time.

In an application with high angular velocities, we recommend a high convergence
speed, so the errors of the gyroscope can be corrected fast. In applications with
only slow movements we recommend a low convergence speed. You can change the
convergence speed on the fly. So it is possible (and recommended) to increase
the convergence speed before an abrupt movement and decrease it afterwards
again.

You might want to play around with the convergence speed in the Brick Viewer to
get a feeling for a good value for your application.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_convergence_speed
documentation: Returns the convergence speed as set by {func}`Set Convergence Speed`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.set_calibration
documentation:
There are several different types that can be calibrated:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Type", "Description", "Values"
 :widths: 10, 30, 110

 "0",    "Accelerometer Gain", "``[mul x, mul y, mul z, div x, div y, div z, 0, 0, 0, 0]``"
 "1",    "Accelerometer Bias", "``[bias x, bias y, bias z, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]``"
 "2",    "Magnetometer Gain",  "``[mul x, mul y, mul z, div x, div y, div z, 0, 0, 0, 0]``"
 "3",    "Magnetometer Bias",  "``[bias x, bias y, bias z, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]``"
 "4",    "Gyroscope Gain",     "``[mul x, mul y, mul z, div x, div y, div z, 0, 0, 0, 0]``"
 "5",    "Gyroscope Bias",     "``[bias xl, bias yl, bias zl, temp l, bias xh, bias yh, bias zh, temp h, 0, 0]``"
```

The calibration via gain and bias is done with the following formula:

```
new_value = (bias + orig_value) * gain_mul / gain_div
```

If you really want to write your own calibration software, please keep
in mind that you first have to undo the old calibration (set bias to 0 and
gain to 1/1) and that you have to average over several thousand values
to obtain a usable result in the end.

The gyroscope bias is highly dependent on the temperature, so you have to
calibrate the bias two times with different temperatures. The values `xl`,
`yl`, `zl` and `temp l` are the bias for `x`, `y`, `z` and the
corresponding temperature for a low temperature. The values `xh`, `yh`,
`zh` and `temp h` are the same for a high temperatures. The temperature
difference should be at least 5°C. If you have a temperature where the
IMU Brick is mostly used, you should use this temperature for one of the
sampling points.

:::{note}
We highly recommend that you use the Brick Viewer to calibrate your
IMU Brick.
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_calibration
documentation: Returns the calibration for a given type as set by {func}`Set Calibration`.

documentation:

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_angular_velocity_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Angular Velocity Period`.

Sets the period with which the {cb}`All Data` callback is triggered

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_quaternion_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Quaternion Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.orientation_calculation_on
documentation:
Turns the orientation calculation of the IMU Brick on.

As default the calculation is on.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.orientation_calculation_off
documentation:
Turns the orientation calculation of the IMU Brick off.

If the calculation is off, {func}`Get Orientation` will return
the last calculated value until the calculation is turned on again.

The trigonometric functions that are needed to calculate the orientation
are very expensive. We recommend to turn the orientation calculation
off if the orientation is not needed, to free calculation time for the
sensor fusion algorithm.

As default the calculation is on.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.is_orientation_calculation_on
documentation:
Returns *true* if the orientation calculation of the IMU Brick
is on, *false* otherwise.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.set_spitfp_baudrate_config
documentation:
The SPITF protocol can be used with a dynamic baudrate. If the dynamic baudrate is
enabled, the Brick will try to adapt the baudrate for the communication
between Bricks and Bricklets according to the amount of data that is transferred.

The baudrate will be increased exponentially if lots of data is sent/received and
decreased linearly if little data is sent/received.

This lowers the baudrate in applications where little data is transferred (e.g.
a weather station) and increases the robustness. If there is lots of data to transfer
(e.g. Thermal Imaging Bricklet) it automatically increases the baudrate as needed.

In cases where some data has to transferred as fast as possible every few seconds
(e.g. RS485 Bricklet with a high baudrate but small payload) you may want to turn
the dynamic baudrate off to get the highest possible performance.

The maximum value of the baudrate can be set per port with the function
{func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate`. If the dynamic baudrate is disabled, the baudrate
as set by {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate` will be used statically.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.5$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_spitfp_baudrate_config
documentation:
Returns the baudrate config, see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate Config`.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.5$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_send_timeout_count
documentation:
Returns the timeout count for the different communication methods.

The methods 0-2 are available for all Bricks, 3-7 only for Master Bricks.

This function is mostly used for debugging during development, in normal operation
the counters should nearly always stay at 0.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.3$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.set_spitfp_baudrate
documentation:
Sets the baudrate for a specific Bricklet port.

If you want to increase the throughput of Bricklets you can increase
the baudrate. If you get a high error count because of high
interference (see {func}`Get SPITFP Error Count`) you can decrease the
baudrate.

If the dynamic baudrate feature is enabled, the baudrate set by this
function corresponds to the maximum baudrate (see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate Config`).

Regulatory testing is done with the default baudrate. If CE compatibility
or similar is necessary in your applications we recommend to not change
the baudrate.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.3$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_spitfp_baudrate
documentation:
Returns the baudrate for a given Bricklet port, see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate`.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.3$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

- ACK checksum errors,
- message checksum errors,
- framing errors and
- overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Brick side. All
Bricklets have a similar function that returns the errors on the Bricklet side.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.3$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.enable_status_led
documentation:
Enables the status LED.

The status LED is the blue LED next to the USB connector. If enabled is is
on and it flickers if data is transfered. If disabled it is always off.

The default state is enabled.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.disable_status_led
documentation:
Disables the status LED.

The status LED is the blue LED next to the USB connector. If enabled is is
on and it flickers if data is transfered. If disabled it is always off.

The default state is enabled.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.is_status_led_enabled
documentation:
Returns *true* if the status LED is enabled, *false* otherwise.

:::{versionadded} 2.3.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_protocol1_bricklet_name
documentation:
Returns the firmware and protocol version and the name of the Bricklet for a
given port.

This functions sole purpose is to allow automatic flashing of v1.x.y Bricklet
plugins.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has an
accuracy of ±15%. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Brick. Calling this function
on a Brick inside of a stack will reset the whole stack.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.write_bricklet_plugin
documentation:
Writes 32 bytes of firmware to the bricklet attached at the given port.
The bytes are written to the position offset * 32.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.read_bricklet_plugin
documentation:
Reads 32 bytes of firmware from the bricklet attached at the given port.
The bytes are read starting at the position offset * 32.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Brick is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position is the position in the stack from '0' (bottom) to '8' (top).

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu.BrickIMU.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_all_values
documentation:
Returns all values measured by the CO2 Bricklet 2.0.

If you want to get the values periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`All Values` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set All Values Callback Configuration`.

:::{note}
The sensor is able to messure up to 120 °C. However it is only specified up to 70 °C.
Exposing the Bricklet to higher temperatures might result in permanent damage.
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.set_air_pressure
documentation:
The CO2 concentration depends (among other things) on the ambient air pressure.

To increase the accuracy of the CO2 Bricklet 2.0 you can set the current air pressure.
You use the {ref}`Barometer Bricklet 2.0 <barometer_v2_bricklet>` or the
{ref}`Air Quality Bricklet <air_quality_bricklet>` to get the current air pressure.

By default air pressure compensation is disabled. Once you set a value it
will be used for compensation. You can turn the compensation off again by
setting the value to 0.

It is sufficient to update the value every few minutes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_air_pressure
documentation: Returns the ambient air pressure as set by {func}`Set Air Pressure`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.set_temperature_offset
documentation:
Sets a temperature offset. A offset of 10 will decrease
the measured temperature by 0.1 °C.

If you install this Bricklet into an enclosure and you want to measure the ambient
temperature, you may have to decrease the measured temperature by some value to
compensate for the error because of the heating inside of the enclosure.

We recommend that you leave the parts in the enclosure running for at least
24 hours such that a temperature equilibrium can be reached. After that you can measure
the temperature directly outside of enclosure and set the difference as offset.

This temperature offset is used to calculate the relative humidity and
CO2 concentration. In case the Bricklet is installed in an enclosure, we
recommend to measure and set the temperature offset to improve the accuracy of
the measurements.

It is sufficient to set the temperature offset once. The offset is saved in
non-volatile memory and is applied again after a power loss.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_temperature_offset
documentation:
Returns the temperature offset as set by
{func}`Set Temperature Offset`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.set_all_values_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`All Values`
callback is triggered periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after at least one of the values has changed. If the values didn't
change within the period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_all_values_callback_configuration
documentation:
Returns the callback configuration as set by
{func}`Set All Values Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_co2_concentration
documentation:
Returns CO2 concentration.

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`CO2 Concentration` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set CO2 Concentration Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.set_co2_concentration_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`CO2 Concentration` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change
within the period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

It is furthermore possible to constrain the callback with thresholds.

The `option`-parameter together with min/max sets a threshold for the {cb}`CO2 Concentration` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Threshold is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *inside* or equal to the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

If the option is set to 'x' (threshold turned off) the callback is triggered with the fixed period.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_co2_concentration_callback_configuration
documentation: Returns the callback configuration as set by {func}`Set CO2 Concentration Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_temperature
documentation:
Returns temperature.

:::{note}
The sensor is able to messure up to 120 °C. However it is only specified up to 70 °C.
Exposing the Bricklet to higher temperatures might result in permanent damage.
:::

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Temperature` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Temperature Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.set_temperature_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Temperature` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change
within the period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

It is furthermore possible to constrain the callback with thresholds.

The `option`-parameter together with min/max sets a threshold for the {cb}`Temperature` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Threshold is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *inside* or equal to the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

If the option is set to 'x' (threshold turned off) the callback is triggered with the fixed period.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_temperature_callback_configuration
documentation: Returns the callback configuration as set by {func}`Set Temperature Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_humidity
documentation:
Returns relative humidity.

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Humidity` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set Humidity Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.set_humidity_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Humidity` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change
within the period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

It is furthermore possible to constrain the callback with thresholds.

The `option`-parameter together with min/max sets a threshold for the {cb}`Humidity` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Threshold is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *inside* or equal to the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

If the option is set to 'x' (threshold turned off) the callback is triggered with the fixed period.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_humidity_callback_configuration
documentation: Returns the callback configuration as set by {func}`Set Humidity Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_co2_v2.BrickletCO2V2.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

documentation: internal
Measures CO2 concentration, temperature and humidity

was detected can be adjusted with one of the small potentiometers on the

<motion_detector_bricklet_sensitivity_delay_block_time>`.

There is also a blue LED on the Bricklet that is on as long as the Bricklet is
in the "motion detected" state.

classes.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_acceleration
documentation:
Returns the calibrated acceleration from the accelerometer for the
x, y and z axis. The acceleration is in the range configured with
{func}`Set Sensor Configuration`.

If you want to get the acceleration periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Acceleration` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Acceleration Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_magnetic_field
documentation:
Returns the calibrated magnetic field from the magnetometer for the
x, y and z axis.

If you want to get the magnetic field periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Magnetic Field` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Magnetic Field Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_angular_velocity
documentation:
Returns the calibrated angular velocity from the gyroscope for the
x, y and z axis. The angular velocity is in the range configured with
{func}`Set Sensor Configuration`.

If you want to get the angular velocity periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Angular Velocity` acallback nd set the period with
{func}`Set Angular Velocity Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature of the IMU Brick.
The temperature is measured in the core of the BNO055 IC, it is not the
ambient temperature

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_orientation
documentation:
Returns the current orientation (heading, roll, pitch) of the IMU Brick as
independent Euler angles. Note that Euler angles always
experience a [gimbal lock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gimbal_lock).
We recommend that you use quaternions instead, if you need the absolute
orientation.

If you want to get the orientation periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Orientation` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Orientation Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_linear_acceleration
documentation:
Returns the linear acceleration of the IMU Brick for the
x, y and z axis. The acceleration is in the range configured with
{func}`Set Sensor Configuration`.

The linear acceleration is the acceleration in each of the three
axis of the IMU Brick with the influences of gravity removed.

It is also possible to get the gravity vector with the influence of linear
acceleration removed, see {func}`Get Gravity Vector`.

If you want to get the linear acceleration periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Linear Acceleration` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Linear Acceleration Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_gravity_vector
documentation:
Returns the current gravity vector of the IMU Brick for the
x, y and z axis.

The gravity vector is the acceleration that occurs due to gravity.
Influences of additional linear acceleration are removed.

It is also possible to get the linear acceleration with the influence
of gravity removed, see {func}`Get Linear Acceleration`.

If you want to get the gravity vector periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Gravity Vector` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Gravity Vector Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_quaternion
documentation:
Returns the current orientation (w, x, y, z) of the IMU Brick as
[quaternions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quaternions_and_spatial_rotation).

You have to divide the return values by 16383 (14 bit) to get
the usual range of -1.0 to +1.0 for quaternions.

If you want to get the quaternions periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`Quaternion` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set Quaternion Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_all_data
documentation:
Return all of the available data of the IMU Brick.

- acceleration (see {func}`Get Acceleration`)
- magnetic field (see {func}`Get Magnetic Field`)
- angular velocity (see {func}`Get Angular Velocity`)
- Euler angles (see {func}`Get Orientation`)
- quaternion (see {func}`Get Quaternion`)
- linear acceleration (see {func}`Get Linear Acceleration`)
- gravity vector (see {func}`Get Gravity Vector`)
- temperature (see {func}`Get Temperature`)
- calibration status (see below)

The calibration status consists of four pairs of two bits. Each pair
of bits represents the status of the current calibration.

- bit 0-1: Magnetometer
- bit 2-3: Accelerometer
- bit 4-5: Gyroscope
- bit 6-7: System

A value of 0 means for "not calibrated" and a value of 3 means
"fully calibrated". In your program you should always be able to
ignore the calibration status, it is used by the calibration
window of the Brick Viewer and it can be ignored after the first
calibration. See the documentation in the calibration window for
more information regarding the calibration of the IMU Brick.

If you want to get the data periodically, it is recommended
to use the {cb}`All Data` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set All Data Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.leds_on
documentation: Turns the orientation and direction LEDs of the IMU Brick on.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.leds_off
documentation: Turns the orientation and direction LEDs of the IMU Brick off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.are_leds_on
documentation:
Returns *true* if the orientation and direction LEDs of the IMU Brick
are on, *false* otherwise.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.save_calibration
documentation:
A call of this function saves the current calibration to be used
as a starting point for the next restart of continuous calibration
of the IMU Brick.

A return value of *true* means that the calibration could be used and
*false* means that it could not be used (this happens if the calibration
status is not "fully calibrated").

This function is used by the calibration window of the Brick Viewer, you
should not need to call it in your program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_acceleration_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Acceleration` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_acceleration_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Acceleration Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_magnetic_field_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Magnetic Field` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_magnetic_field_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Magnetic Field Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_angular_velocity_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Angular Velocity` callback is
triggered periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_angular_velocity_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Angular Velocity Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_temperature_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Temperature` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_temperature_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Temperature Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_orientation_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Orientation` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_orientation_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Orientation Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_linear_acceleration_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Linear Acceleration` callback is
triggered periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_linear_acceleration_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Linear Acceleration Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_gravity_vector_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Gravity Vector` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_gravity_vector_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Gravity Vector Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_quaternion_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Quaternion` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_quaternion_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Quaternion Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_all_data_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`All Data` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_all_data_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set All Data Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_sensor_configuration
documentation:
Sets the available sensor configuration for the Magnetometer, Gyroscope and
Accelerometer. The Accelerometer Range is user selectable in all fusion modes,
all other configurations are auto-controlled in fusion mode.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.5$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_sensor_configuration
documentation:
Returns the sensor configuration as set by {func}`Set Sensor Configuration`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.5$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_sensor_fusion_mode
documentation:
If the fusion mode is turned off, the functions {func}`Get Acceleration`,
{func}`Get Magnetic Field` and {func}`Get Angular Velocity` return uncalibrated
and uncompensated sensor data. All other sensor data getters return no data.

Since firmware version 2.0.6 you can also use a fusion mode without magnetometer.
In this mode the calculated orientation is relative (with magnetometer it is
absolute with respect to the earth). However, the calculation can't be influenced
by spurious magnetic fields.

Since firmware version 2.0.13 you can also use a fusion mode without fast
magnetometer calibration. This mode is the same as the normal fusion mode,
but the fast magnetometer calibration is turned off. So to find the orientation
the first time will likely take longer, but small magnetic influences might
not affect the automatic calibration as much.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.5$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_sensor_fusion_mode
documentation:
Returns the sensor fusion mode as set by {func}`Set Sensor Fusion Mode`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.5$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_spitfp_baudrate_config
documentation:
The SPITF protocol can be used with a dynamic baudrate. If the dynamic baudrate is
enabled, the Brick will try to adapt the baudrate for the communication
between Bricks and Bricklets according to the amount of data that is transferred.

The baudrate will be increased exponentially if lots of data is sent/received and
decreased linearly if little data is sent/received.

This lowers the baudrate in applications where little data is transferred (e.g.
a weather station) and increases the robustness. If there is lots of data to transfer
(e.g. Thermal Imaging Bricklet) it automatically increases the baudrate as needed.

In cases where some data has to transferred as fast as possible every few seconds
(e.g. RS485 Bricklet with a high baudrate but small payload) you may want to turn
the dynamic baudrate off to get the highest possible performance.

The maximum value of the baudrate can be set per port with the function
{func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate`. If the dynamic baudrate is disabled, the baudrate
as set by {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate` will be used statically.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.10$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_spitfp_baudrate_config
documentation:
Returns the baudrate config, see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate Config`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.10$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_send_timeout_count
documentation:
Returns the timeout count for the different communication methods.

The methods 0-2 are available for all Bricks, 3-7 only for Master Bricks.

This function is mostly used for debugging during development, in normal operation
the counters should nearly always stay at 0.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.7$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.set_spitfp_baudrate
documentation:
Sets the baudrate for a specific Bricklet port.

If you want to increase the throughput of Bricklets you can increase
the baudrate. If you get a high error count because of high
interference (see {func}`Get SPITFP Error Count`) you can decrease the
baudrate.

If the dynamic baudrate feature is enabled, the baudrate set by this
function corresponds to the maximum baudrate (see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate Config`).

Regulatory testing is done with the default baudrate. If CE compatibility
or similar is necessary in your applications we recommend to not change
the baudrate.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.5$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_spitfp_baudrate
documentation:
Returns the baudrate for a given Bricklet port, see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.5$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

- ACK checksum errors,
- message checksum errors,
- framing errors and
- overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Brick side. All
Bricklets have a similar function that returns the errors on the Bricklet side.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.5$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.enable_status_led
documentation:
Enables the status LED.

The status LED is the blue LED next to the USB connector. If enabled is is
on and it flickers if data is transfered. If disabled it is always off.

The default state is enabled.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.disable_status_led
documentation:
Disables the status LED.

The status LED is the blue LED next to the USB connector. If enabled is is
on and it flickers if data is transfered. If disabled it is always off.

The default state is enabled.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.is_status_led_enabled
documentation: Returns *true* if the status LED is enabled, *false* otherwise.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_protocol1_bricklet_name
documentation:
Returns the firmware and protocol version and the name of the Bricklet for a
given port.

This functions sole purpose is to allow automatic flashing of v1.x.y Bricklet
plugins.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has an
accuracy of ±15%. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Brick. Calling this function
on a Brick inside of a stack will reset the whole stack.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.write_bricklet_plugin
documentation:
Writes 32 bytes of firmware to the bricklet attached at the given port.
The bytes are written to the position offset * 32.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.read_bricklet_plugin
documentation:
Reads 32 bytes of firmware from the bricklet attached at the given port.
The bytes are read starting at the position offset * 32.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Brick is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position is the position in the stack from '0' (bottom) to '8' (top).

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

class_variables.name: tinkerforge.brick_imu_v2.BrickIMUV2.DEVICE_URL_PART
documentation: internal
Full fledged AHRS with 9 degrees of freedom

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.EnergyMonitor
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_energy_data
documentation:
Returns all of the measurements that are done by the Energy Monitor Bricklet.

* Voltage RMS
* Current RMS
* Energy (integrated over time)
* Real Power
* Apparent Power
* Reactive Power
* Power Factor
* Frequency (AC Frequency of the mains voltage)

The frequency is recalculated every 6 seconds.

All other values are integrated over 10 zero-crossings of the voltage sine wave.
With a standard AC mains voltage frequency of 50Hz this results in a 5 measurements
per second (or an integration time of 200ms per measurement).

If no voltage transformer is connected, the Bricklet will use the current waveform
to calculate the frequency and it will use an integration time of
10 zero-crossings of the current waveform.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.reset_energy
documentation: Sets the energy value (see {func}`Get Energy Data`) back to 0Wh.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_waveform_low_level
documentation:
Returns a snapshot of the voltage and current waveform. The values
in the returned array alternate between voltage and current. The data from
one getter call contains 768 data points for voltage and current, which
correspond to about 3 full sine waves.

The voltage is given with a resolution of 100mV and the current is given
with a resolution of 10mA.

This data is meant to be used for a non-realtime graphical representation of
the voltage and current waveforms.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_transformer_status
documentation: Returns *true* if a voltage/current transformer is connected to the Bricklet.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.set_transformer_calibration
documentation:
Sets the transformer ratio for the voltage and current transformer in 1/100 form.

Example: If your mains voltage is 230V, you use 9V voltage transformer and a
1V:30A current clamp your voltage ratio is 230/9 = 25.56 and your current ratio
is 30/1 = 30.

In this case you have to set the values 2556 and 3000 for voltage ratio and current
ratio.

The calibration is saved in non-volatile memory, you only have to set it once.

Set the phase shift to 0. It is for future use and currently not supported by the Bricklet.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_transformer_calibration
documentation: Returns the transformer calibration as set by {func}`Set Transformer Calibration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.calibrate_offset
documentation:
Calling this function will start an offset calibration. The offset calibration will
integrate the voltage and current waveform over a longer time period to find the 0
transition point in the sine wave.

The Bricklet comes with a factory-calibrated offset value, you should not have to
call this function.

If you want to re-calibrate the offset we recommend that you connect a load that
has a smooth sinusoidal voltage and current waveform. Alternatively you can also
short both inputs.

The calibration is saved in non-volatile memory, you only have to set it once.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.set_energy_data_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Energy Data`
callback is triggered periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change within the
period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_energy_data_callback_configuration
documentation:
Returns the callback configuration as set by
{func}`Set Energy Data Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.get_waveform
documentation:
Returns a snapshot of the voltage and current waveform. The values
in the returned array alternate between voltage and current. The data from
one getter call contains 768 data points for voltage and current, which
correspond to about 3 full sine waves.

The voltage is given with a resolution of 100mV and the current is given
with a resolution of 10mA.

This data is meant to be used for a non-realtime graphical representation of
the voltage and current waveforms.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_energy_monitor.BrickletEnergyMonitor.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

variables.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.HATZero
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.get_usb_voltage
documentation:
Returns the USB supply voltage of the Raspberry Pi.

If you want to get the value periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`USB Voltage` callback. You can set the callback configuration
with {func}`Set USB Voltage Callback Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.set_usb_voltage_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`USB Voltage` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change
within the period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

It is furthermore possible to constrain the callback with thresholds.

The `option`-parameter together with min/max sets a threshold for the {cb}`USB Voltage` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Threshold is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is *inside* or equal to the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Threshold is triggered when the value is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

If the option is set to 'x' (threshold turned off) the callback is triggered with the fixed period.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.get_usb_voltage_callback_configuration
documentation:
Returns the callback configuration as set by {func}`Set USB Voltage Callback Configuration`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the HAT is connected to
(typically '0' as the HAT is the root device in the topology),
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The HAT (Zero) Brick is always at position 'i'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

class_variables.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat_zero.BrickHATZero.DEVICE_URL_PART
documentation: internal
HAT for Raspberry Pi Zero with 4 Bricklets ports

documentation: Returns statistics for the Isolator Bricklet.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_isolator.BrickletIsolator.set_spitfp_baudrate_config
documentation:
The SPITF protocol can be used with a dynamic baudrate. If the dynamic baudrate is
enabled, the Isolator Bricklet will try to adapt the baudrate for the communication
between Bricks and Bricklets according to the amount of data that is transferred.

The baudrate for communication config between
Brick and Isolator Bricklet can be set through the API of the Brick.

The baudrate will be increased exponentially if lots of data is sent/received and
decreased linearly if little data is sent/received.

This lowers the baudrate in applications where little data is transferred (e.g.
a weather station) and increases the robustness. If there is lots of data to transfer
(e.g. Thermal Imaging Bricklet) it automatically increases the baudrate as needed.

In cases where some data has to transferred as fast as possible every few seconds
(e.g. RS485 Bricklet with a high baudrate but small payload) you may want to turn
the dynamic baudrate off to get the highest possible performance.

The maximum value of the baudrate can be set per port with the function
{func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate`. If the dynamic baudrate is disabled, the baudrate
as set by {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate` will be used statically.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_isolator.BrickletIsolator.get_spitfp_baudrate_config
documentation: Returns the baudrate config, see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate Config`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_isolator.BrickletIsolator.set_spitfp_baudrate
documentation:
Sets the baudrate for a the communication between Isolator Bricklet
and the connected Bricklet. The baudrate for communication between
Brick and Isolator Bricklet can be set through the API of the Brick.

If you want to increase the throughput of Bricklets you can increase
the baudrate. If you get a high error count because of high
interference (see {func}`Get SPITFP Error Count`) you can decrease the
baudrate.

If the dynamic baudrate feature is enabled, the baudrate set by this
function corresponds to the maximum baudrate (see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate Config`).

Regulatory testing is done with the default baudrate. If CE compatibility
or similar is necessary in your applications we recommend to not change
the baudrate.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_isolator.BrickletIsolator.get_spitfp_baudrate
documentation: Returns the baudrate, see {func}`Set SPITFP Baudrate`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_isolator.BrickletIsolator.get_isolator_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Isolator Bricklet and
the connected Bricklet. Call {func}`Get SPITFP Error Count` to get the
error count between Isolator Bricklet and Brick.

The errors are divided into

- ACK checksum errors,
- message checksum errors,
- framing errors and
- overflow errors.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_isolator.BrickletIsolator.set_statistics_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Statistics`
callback is triggered periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change within the
period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_isolator.BrickletIsolator.get_statistics_callback_configuration

{func}`Set Statistics Callback Configuration`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_isolator.BrickletIsolator.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,

documentation:

documentation:

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_one_wire.BrickletOneWire.search_bus_low_level
documentation:
Returns a list of up to 64 identifiers of the connected 1-Wire devices.
Each identifier is 64-bit and consists of 8-bit family code, 48-bit ID and
8-bit CRC.

To get these identifiers the Bricklet runs the
`SEARCH ROM algorithm <https://www.maximintegrated.com/en/app-notes/index.mvp/id/187>`__,
as defined by Maxim.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_one_wire.BrickletOneWire.reset_bus
documentation: Resets the bus with the 1-Wire reset operation.

documentation: Writes a byte of data to the 1-Wire bus.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_one_wire.BrickletOneWire.read
documentation: Reads a byte of data from the 1-Wire bus.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_one_wire.BrickletOneWire.write_command
documentation:
Writes a command to the 1-Wire device with the given identifier. You can obtain
the identifier by calling {func}`Search Bus`. The MATCH ROM operation is used to
write the command.

If you only have one device connected or want to broadcast to all devices
you can set the identifier to 0. In this case the SKIP ROM operation is used to
write the command.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_piezo_buzzer.BrickletPiezoBuzzer.morse_code
documentation:
Sets morse code that will be played by the piezo buzzer. The morse code
is given as a string consisting of "." (dot), "-" (minus) and " " (space)
for *dits*, *dahs* and *pauses*. Every other character is ignored.

For example: If you set the string "...---...", the piezo buzzer will beep
nine times with the durations "short short short long long long short
short short".

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_uv_light.BrickletUVLight.get_uv_light
documentation:
Returns the UV light intensity of the sensor.
The sensor has already weighted the intensity with the erythemal
action spectrum to get the skin-affecting irradiation.

To get UV index you just have to divide the value by 250. For example, a UV
light intensity of 500 is equivalent to an UV index of 2.

If you want to get the intensity periodically, it is recommended to use the
{cb}`UV Light` callback and set the period with
{func}`Set UV Light Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_uv_light.BrickletUVLight.set_uv_light_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`UV Light` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`UV Light` callback is only triggered if the intensity has changed since
the last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_uv_light.BrickletUVLight.get_uv_light_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set UV Light Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_uv_light.BrickletUVLight.set_uv_light_callback_threshold
documentation:
Sets the thresholds for the {cb}`UV Light Reached` callback.

The following options are possible:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Option", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "'x'",    "Callback is turned off"
 "'o'",    "Callback is triggered when the intensity is *outside* the min and max values"
 "'i'",    "Callback is triggered when the intensity is *inside* the min and max values"
 "'<'",    "Callback is triggered when the intensity is smaller than the min value (max is ignored)"
 "'>'",    "Callback is triggered when the intensity is greater than the min value (max is ignored)"
```

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_uv_light.BrickletUVLight.set_debounce_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the threshold callbacks

- {cb}`UV Light Reached`,

are triggered, if the thresholds

- {func}`Set UV Light Callback Threshold`,

keep being reached.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_uv_light.BrickletUVLight.get_debounce_period
documentation: Returns the debounce period as set by {func}`Set Debounce Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_uv_light.BrickletUVLight.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.RemoteSwitch
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch.switch_socket
documentation: This function is deprecated, use {func}`Switch Socket A` instead.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch.get_switching_state
documentation:
Returns the current switching state. If the current state is busy, the
Bricklet is currently sending a code to switch a socket. It will not
accept any requests to switch sockets until the state changes to ready.

How long the switching takes is dependent on the number of repeats, see
{func}`Set Repeats`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch.set_repeats
documentation:
Sets the number of times the code is sent when one of the switch socket
functions is called. The repeats basically correspond to the amount of time
that a button of the remote is pressed.

Some dimmers are controlled by the length of a button pressed,
this can be simulated by increasing the repeats.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch.get_repeats
documentation: Returns the number of repeats as set by {func}`Set Repeats`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch.switch_socket_a
documentation:
To switch a type A socket you have to give the house code, receiver code and the
state (on or off) you want to switch to.

A detailed description on how you can figure out the house and receiver code
can be found {ref}`here <remote_switch_bricklet_type_a_house_and_receiver_code>`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch.switch_socket_b
documentation:
To switch a type B socket you have to give the address, unit and the state
(on or off) you want to switch to.

To switch all devices with the same address use 255 for the unit.

A detailed description on how you can teach a socket the address and unit can
be found {ref}`here <remote_switch_bricklet_type_b_address_and_unit>`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch.dim_socket_b
documentation:
To control a type B dimmer you have to give the address, unit and the
dim value you want to set the dimmer to.

A detailed description on how you can teach a dimmer the address and unit can
be found {ref}`here <remote_switch_bricklet_type_b_address_and_unit>`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_remote_switch.BrickletRemoteSwitch.switch_socket_c
documentation:
To switch a type C socket you have to give the system code, device code and the
state (on or off) you want to switch to.

A detailed description on how you can figure out the system and device code
can be found {ref}`here <remote_switch_bricklet_type_c_system_and_device_code>`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::
