variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.GPSV3
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_coordinates
documentation:
Returns the GPS coordinates. Latitude and longitude are given in the
`DD.dddddd°` format, the value 57123468 means 57.123468°.
The parameter `ns` and `ew` are the cardinal directions for
latitude and longitude. Possible values for `ns` and `ew` are 'N', 'S', 'E'
and 'W' (north, south, east and west).

This data is only valid if there is currently a fix as indicated by
{func}`Get Status`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_status
documentation:
Returns if a fix is currently available as well as the number of
satellites that are in view.

There is also a {ref}`green LED <gps_v2_bricklet_fix_led>` on the Bricklet that
indicates the fix status.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_altitude
documentation:
Returns the current altitude and corresponding geoidal separation.

This data is only valid if there is currently a fix as indicated by
{func}`Get Status`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_motion
documentation:
Returns the current course and speed. A course of 0° means the Bricklet is
traveling north bound and 90° means it is traveling east bound.

Please note that this only returns useful values if an actual movement
is present.

This data is only valid if there is currently a fix as indicated by
{func}`Get Status`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_date_time
documentation:
Returns the current date and time. The date is
given in the format `ddmmyy` and the time is given
in the format `hhmmss.sss`. For example, 140713 means
14.07.13 as date and 195923568 means 19:59:23.568 as time.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.restart
documentation:
Restarts the GPS Bricklet, the following restart types are available:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Value", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "0", "Hot start (use all available data in the NV store)"
 "1", "Warm start (don't use ephemeris at restart)"
 "2", "Cold start (don't use time, position, almanacs and ephemeris at restart)"
 "3", "Factory reset (clear all system/user configurations at restart)"
```

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_satellite_system_status_low_level
documentation:
Returns the

* satellite numbers list (up to 12 items)
* fix value,
* PDOP value,
* HDOP value and
* VDOP value

for a given satellite system. Currently GPS and GLONASS are supported, Galileo
is not yet supported.

The GPS and GLONASS satellites have unique numbers and the satellite list gives
the numbers of the satellites that are currently utilized. The number 0 is not
a valid satellite number and can be ignored in the list.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_satellite_status
documentation:
Returns the current elevation, azimuth and SNR
for a given satellite and satellite system.

The satellite number here always goes from 1 to 32. For GLONASS it corresponds to
the satellites 65-96.

Galileo is not yet supported.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_fix_led_config
documentation:
Sets the fix LED configuration. By default the LED shows if
the Bricklet got a GPS fix yet. If a fix is established the LED turns on.
If there is no fix then the LED is turned off.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off, show a heartbeat or let it blink
in sync with the PPS (pulse per second) output of the GPS module.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_fix_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Fix LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_coordinates_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Coordinates` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Coordinates` callback is only triggered if the coordinates changed
since the last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_coordinates_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Coordinates Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_status_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Status` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Status` callback is only triggered if the status changed since the
last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_status_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Status Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_altitude_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Altitude` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Altitude` callback is only triggered if the altitude changed since the
last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_altitude_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Altitude Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_motion_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Motion` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Motion` callback is only triggered if the motion changed since the
last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_motion_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Motion Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_date_time_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Date Time` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Date Time` callback is only triggered if the date or time changed
since the last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_date_time_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Date Time Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_sbas_config
documentation:
If `SBAS <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNSS_augmentation#Satellite-based_augmentation_system>`__ is enabled,
the position accuracy increases (if SBAS satellites are in view),
but the update rate is limited to 5Hz. With SBAS disabled the update rate is increased to 10Hz.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_sbas_config
documentation: Returns the SBAS configuration as set by {func}`Set SBAS Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.get_satellite_system_status
documentation:
Returns the

* satellite numbers list (up to 12 items)
* fix value,
* PDOP value,
* HDOP value and
* VDOP value

for a given satellite system. Currently GPS and GLONASS are supported, Galileo
is not yet supported.

The GPS and GLONASS satellites have unique numbers and the satellite list gives
the numbers of the satellites that are currently utilized. The number 0 is not
a valid satellite number and can be ignored in the list.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v3.BrickletGPSV3.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

documentation: internal
Determine position, velocity and altitude using GPS

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_motion_detector_v2.BrickletMotionDetectorV2.get_motion_detected
documentation:
Returns 1 if a motion was detected. It returns 1 approx. for 1.8 seconds
until the sensor checks for a new movement.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_motion_detector_v2.BrickletMotionDetectorV2.set_sensitivity
documentation:
Sets the sensitivity of the PIR sensor. At full
sensitivity (100), the Bricklet can detect motion in a range of approximately 12m.

The actual range depends on many things in the environment (e.g. reflections) and the
size of the object to be detected. While a big person might be detected in a range
of 10m a cat may only be detected at 2m distance with the same setting.

So you will have to find a good sensitivity for your application by trial and error.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_motion_detector_v2.BrickletMotionDetectorV2.get_sensitivity
documentation: Returns the sensitivity as set by {func}`Set Sensitivity`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_motion_detector_v2.BrickletMotionDetectorV2.set_indicator
documentation:
Sets the blue backlight of the fresnel lens. The backlight consists of
three LEDs. The brightness of each LED can be controlled with a 8-bit value
(0-255). A value of 0 turns the LED off and a value of 255 turns the LED
to full brightness.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_motion_detector_v2.BrickletMotionDetectorV2.get_indicator
documentation: Returns the indicator configuration as set by {func}`Set Indicator`.

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.LCD20x4
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.write_line
documentation:
Writes text to a specific line with a specific position.
The text can have a maximum of 20 characters.

For example: (0, 7, "Hello") will write *Hello* in the middle of the
first line of the display.

The display uses a special charset that includes all ASCII characters except
backslash and tilde. The LCD charset also includes several other non-ASCII characters, see
the `charset specification <https://github.com/Tinkerforge/lcd-20x4-bricklet/raw/master/datasheets/standard_charset.pdf>`__
for details. The Unicode example above shows how to specify non-ASCII characters
and how to translate from Unicode to the LCD charset.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.clear_display
documentation: Deletes all characters from the display.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.backlight_on
documentation: Turns the backlight on.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.backlight_off
documentation: Turns the backlight off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.is_backlight_on
documentation: Returns *true* if the backlight is on and *false* otherwise.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.set_config
documentation:
Configures if the cursor (shown as "\_") should be visible and if it
should be blinking (shown as a blinking block). The cursor position
is one character behind the the last text written with
{func}`Write Line`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.get_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Config`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.is_button_pressed
documentation:
Returns *true* if the button (0 to 2 or 0 to 3 since hardware version 1.2)
is pressed.

If you want to react on button presses and releases it is recommended to use
the {cb}`Button Pressed` and {cb}`Button Released` callbacks.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.set_custom_character
documentation:
The LCD 20x4 Bricklet can store up to 8 custom characters. The characters
consist of 5x8 pixels and can be addressed with the index 0-7. To describe
the pixels, the first 5 bits of 8 bytes are used. For example, to make
a custom character "H", you should transfer the following:

- `character[0] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[1] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[2] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[3] = 0b00011111` (decimal value 31)
- `character[4] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[5] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[6] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[7] = 0b00000000` (decimal value 0)

The characters can later be written with {func}`Write Line` by using the
characters with the byte representation 8 ("\\x08" or "\\u0008") to 15
("\\x0F" or "\\u000F").

You can play around with the custom characters in Brick Viewer version
since 2.0.1.

Custom characters are stored by the LCD in RAM, so they have to be set
after each startup.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.get_custom_character
documentation:
Returns the custom character for a given index, as set with
{func}`Set Custom Character`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.set_default_text
documentation:
Sets the default text for lines 0-3. The max number of characters
per line is 20.

The default text is shown on the LCD, if the default text counter
expires, see {func}`Set Default Text Counter`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.get_default_text
documentation:
Returns the default text for a given line (0-3) as set by
{func}`Set Default Text`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.set_default_text_counter
documentation:
Sets the default text counter. This counter is decremented each
ms by the LCD firmware. If the counter reaches 0, the default text
(see {func}`Set Default Text`) is shown on the LCD.

This functionality can be used to show a default text if the controlling
program crashes or the connection is interrupted.

A possible approach is to call {func}`Set Default Text Counter` every
minute with the parameter 1000\*60\*2 (2 minutes). In this case the
default text will be shown no later than 2 minutes after the
controlling program crashes.

A negative counter turns the default text functionality off.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.get_default_text_counter
documentation:
Returns the current value of the default text counter.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

class_variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_20x4.BrickletLCD20x4.DEVICE_URL_PART
documentation: internal
20x4 character alphanumeric display with blue backlight

variables.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.HAT
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.set_sleep_mode
documentation:
Sets the sleep mode.

:::{note}
Calling this function will cut the Raspberry Pi's power after Power Off Delay seconds.
You have to shut down the operating system yourself, e.g. by calling 'sudo shutdown -h now'.
:::

Parameters:

- Power Off Delay: Time before the RPi/Bricklets are powered off.
- Power Off Duration: Duration that the RPi/Bricklets stay powered off.
- Raspberry Pi Off: RPi is powered off if set to true.
- Bricklets Off: Bricklets are powered off if set to true.
- Enable Sleep Indicator: If set to true, the status LED will blink in a 1s interval
  during the whole power off duration. This will draw additional 0.3mA.

Example: To turn RPi and Bricklets off in 5 seconds for 10 minutes with sleep
indicator enabled, call (5, 60\*10, true, true, true).

This function can also be used to implement a watchdog. To do this you can
write a program that calls this function once per second in a loop with
(10, 2, true, false, false). If the RPi crashes or gets stuck
the HAT will reset the RPi after 10 seconds.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_sleep_mode
documentation: Returns the sleep mode settings as set by {func}`Set Sleep Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.set_bricklet_power
documentation: Set to true/false to turn the power supply of the connected Bricklets on/off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_bricklet_power
documentation: Returns the power status of the connected Bricklets as set by {func}`Set Bricklet Power`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_voltages
documentation:
Returns the USB supply voltage and the DC input supply voltage.

There are three possible combinations:

* Only USB connected: The USB supply voltage will be fed back to the
  DC input connector. You will read the USB voltage and a slightly lower
  voltage on the DC input.
* Only DC input connected: The DC voltage will not be fed back to the
  USB connector. You will read the DC input voltage and the USB voltage
  will be 0.
* USB and DC input connected: You will read both voltages. In this case
  the USB supply will be without load, but it will work as backup if you
  disconnect the DC input (or if the DC input voltage falls below the
  USB voltage).

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.set_voltages_callback_configuration
documentation:
The period is the period with which the {cb}`Voltages`
callback is triggered periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

If the `value has to change`-parameter is set to true, the callback is only
triggered after the value has changed. If the value didn't change within the
period, the callback is triggered immediately on change.

If it is set to false, the callback is continuously triggered with the period,
independent of the value.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_voltages_callback_configuration
documentation:
Returns the callback configuration as set by
{func}`Set Voltages Callback Configuration`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.set_rtc_driver
documentation:
Configures the RTC driver that is given to the Raspberry Pi to be used.
Currently there are two different RTCs used:

- Hardware version \<= 1.5: PCF8523
- Hardware version 1.6: DS1338

The correct driver will be set during factory flashing by Tinkerforge.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.3$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_rtc_driver
documentation:
Returns the RTC driver as set by {func}`Set RTC Driver`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.3$nbsp;(Firmware)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the HAT is connected to
(typically '0' as the HAT is the root device in the topology),
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The HAT (Zero) Brick is always at position 'i'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

class_variables.name: tinkerforge.brick_hat.BrickHAT.DEVICE_URL_PART
documentation: internal
HAT for Raspberry Pi with 8 Bricklets ports and real-time clock

documentation:

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_counter.BrickletIndustrialCounter.get_signal_data
documentation:

given channel.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_counter.BrickletIndustrialCounter.get_all_signal_data
documentation:

channels.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_counter.BrickletIndustrialCounter.set_counter_active
documentation:
Activates/deactivates the counter of the given channel.

true = activate, false = deactivate.

By default all channels are activated.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_counter.BrickletIndustrialCounter.set_all_counter_active
documentation:
Activates/deactivates the counter of all four channels.

true = activate, false = deactivate.

By default all channels are activated.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_counter.BrickletIndustrialCounter.get_counter_active
documentation:
Returns the activation state of the given channel.

true = activated, false = deactivated.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_counter.BrickletIndustrialCounter.get_all_counter_active
documentation:

true = activated, false = deactivated.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_counter.BrickletIndustrialCounter.set_counter_configuration
documentation:
Sets the counter configuration for the given channel.

* Count Edge: Counter can count on rising, falling or both edges.
* Count Direction: Counter can count up or down. You can also use
  another channel as direction input, see
  `here <https://www.tinkerforge.com/en/doc/Hardware/Bricklets/Industrial_Counter.html#external-count-direction>`__
  for details.
* Duty Cycle Prescaler: Sets a divider for the internal clock. See
  `here <https://www.tinkerforge.com/en/doc/Hardware/Bricklets/Industrial_Counter.html#duty-cycle-prescaler-and-frequency-integration-time>`__
  for details.
* Frequency Integration Time: Sets the integration time for the
  frequency measurement. See
  `here <https://www.tinkerforge.com/en/doc/Hardware/Bricklets/Industrial_Counter.html#duty-cycle-prescaler-and-frequency-integration-time>`__
  for details.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_counter.BrickletIndustrialCounter.get_counter_configuration

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_counter.BrickletIndustrialCounter.set_channel_led_config
documentation:

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
This file was automatically generated on 2024-02-27.      #

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.IO16
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.set_port
documentation:
Sets the output value (high or low) for a port ("a" or "b") with a bitmask
(8bit). A 1 in the bitmask means high and a 0 in the bitmask means low.

For example: The value 15 or 0b00001111 will turn the pins 0-3 high and the
pins 4-7 low for the specified port.

All running monoflop timers of the given port will be aborted if this function
is called.

:::{note}
This function does nothing for pins that are configured as input.
Pull-up resistors can be switched on with {func}`Set Port Configuration`.
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.get_port
documentation:
Returns a bitmask of the values that are currently measured on the
specified port. This function works if the pin is configured to input
as well as if it is configured to output.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.set_port_configuration
documentation:
Configures the value and direction of a specified port. Possible directions
are 'i' and 'o' for input and output.

If the direction is configured as output, the value is either high or low
(set as *true* or *false*).

If the direction is configured as input, the value is either pull-up or
default (set as *true* or *false*).

For example:

* ('a', 255, 'i', true) or ('a', 0b11111111, 'i', true) will set all pins of port A as input pull-up.
* ('a', 128, 'i', false) or ('a', 0b10000000, 'i', false) will set pin 7 of port A as input default (floating if nothing is connected).
* ('b', 3, 'o', false) or ('b', 0b00000011, 'o', false) will set pins 0 and 1 of port B as output low.
* ('b', 4, 'o', true) or ('b', 0b00000100, 'o', true) will set pin 2 of port B as output high.

Running monoflop timers for the selected pins will be aborted if this
function is called.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.get_port_configuration
documentation:
Returns a direction bitmask and a value bitmask for the specified port. A 1 in
the direction bitmask means input and a 0 in the bitmask means output.

For example: A return value of (15, 51) or (0b00001111, 0b00110011) for
direction and value means that:

* pins 0 and 1 are configured as input pull-up,
* pins 2 and 3 are configured as input default,
* pins 4 and 5 are configured as output high
* and pins 6 and 7 are configured as output low.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.set_debounce_period
documentation:
Sets the debounce period of the {cb}`Interrupt` callback.

For example: If you set this value to 100, you will get the interrupt
maximal every 100ms. This is necessary if something that bounces is
connected to the IO-16 Bricklet, such as a button.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.get_debounce_period
documentation: Returns the debounce period as set by {func}`Set Debounce Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.set_port_interrupt
documentation:
Sets the pins on which an interrupt is activated with a bitmask.
Interrupts are triggered on changes of the voltage level of the pin,
i.e. changes from high to low and low to high.

For example: ('a', 129) or ('a', 0b10000001) will enable the interrupt for
pins 0 and 7 of port a.

The interrupt is delivered with the {cb}`Interrupt` callback.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.get_port_interrupt
documentation:
Returns the interrupt bitmask for the specified port as set by
{func}`Set Port Interrupt`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.set_port_monoflop
documentation:
Configures a monoflop of the pins specified by the second parameter as 8 bit
long bitmask. The specified pins must be configured for output. Non-output
pins will be ignored.

The third parameter is a bitmask with the desired value of the specified
output pins. A 1 in the bitmask means high and a 0 in the bitmask means low.

The forth parameter indicates the time that the pins should hold
the value.

If this function is called with the parameters ('a', 9, 1, 1500) or
('a', 0b00001001, 0b00000001, 1500): Pin 0 will get high and pin 3 will get
low on port 'a'. In 1.5s pin 0 will get low and pin 3 will get high again.

A monoflop can be used as a fail-safe mechanism. For example: Lets assume you
have a RS485 bus and an IO-16 Bricklet connected to one of the slave
stacks. You can now call this function every second, with a time parameter
of two seconds and pin 0 set to high. Pin 0 will be high all the time. If now
the RS485 connection is lost, then pin 0 will get low in at most two seconds.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.get_port_monoflop
documentation:
Returns (for the given pin) the current value and the time as set by
{func}`Set Port Monoflop` as well as the remaining time until the value flips.

If the timer is not running currently, the remaining time will be returned
as 0.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.set_selected_values
documentation:
Sets the output value (high or low) for a port ("a" or "b" with a bitmask,
according to the selection mask. The bitmask is 8 bit long and a 1 in the
bitmask means high and a 0 in the bitmask means low.

For example: The parameters ('a', 192, 128) or ('a', 0b11000000, 0b10000000)
will turn pin 7 high and pin 6 low on port A, pins 0-6 will remain untouched.

Running monoflop timers for the selected pins will be aborted if this
function is called.

:::{note}
This function does nothing for pins that are configured as input.
Pull-up resistors can be switched on with {func}`Set Port Configuration`.
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.get_edge_count
documentation:
Returns the current value of the edge counter for the selected pin on port A.
You can configure the edges that are counted with {func}`Set Edge Count Config`.

If you set the reset counter to *true*, the count is set back to 0
directly after it is read.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.3$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.set_edge_count_config
documentation:
Configures the edge counter for the selected pin of port A. Pins 0 and 1
are available for edge counting.

The edge type parameter configures if rising edges, falling edges or
both are counted if the pin is configured for input. Possible edge types are:

- 0 = rising
- 1 = falling
- 2 = both

Configuring an edge counter resets its value to 0.

If you don't know what any of this means, just leave it at default. The
default configuration is very likely OK for you.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.3$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.get_edge_count_config
documentation:
Returns the edge type and debounce time for the selected pin of port A as set by
{func}`Set Edge Count Config`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.3$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_io16.BrickletIO16.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

documentation: internal
16-channel digital input/output

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.OLED128x64V2
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.write_pixels_low_level
documentation:
Writes pixels to the specified window.

The pixels are written into the window line by line top to bottom
and each line is written from left to right.

If automatic draw is enabled (default) the pixels are directly written to
the screen. Only pixels that have actually changed are updated on the screen,
the rest stays the same.

If automatic draw is disabled the pixels are written to an internal buffer and
the buffer is transferred to the display only after {func}`Draw Buffered Frame`
is called. This can be used to avoid flicker when drawing a complex frame in
multiple steps.

Automatic draw can be configured with the {func}`Set Display Configuration`
function.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.read_pixels_low_level
documentation:
Reads pixels from the specified window.

The pixels are read from the window line by line top to bottom
and each line is read from left to right.

If automatic draw is enabled (default) the pixels that are read are always the
same that are shown on the display.

If automatic draw is disabled the pixels are read from the internal buffer
(see {func}`Draw Buffered Frame`).

Automatic draw can be configured with the {func}`Set Display Configuration`
function.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.clear_display
documentation:
Clears the complete content of the display.

If automatic draw is enabled (default) the pixels are directly cleared.

If automatic draw is disabled the the internal buffer is cleared and
the buffer is transferred to the display only after {func}`Draw Buffered Frame`
is called. This can be used to avoid flicker when drawing a complex frame in
multiple steps.

Automatic draw can be configured with the {func}`Set Display Configuration`
function.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.set_display_configuration
documentation:
Sets the configuration of the display.

You can set a contrast value from 0 to 255 and you can invert the color
(white/black) of the display.

If automatic draw is set to *true*, the display is automatically updated with every
call of {func}`Write Pixels` or {func}`Write Line`. If it is set to false, the
changes are written into an internal buffer and only shown on the display after
a call of {func}`Draw Buffered Frame`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.get_display_configuration
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Display Configuration`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.write_line
documentation:
Writes text to a specific line with a specific position.
The text can have a maximum of 22 characters.

For example: (1, 10, "Hello") will write *Hello* in the middle of the
second line of the display.

The display uses a special 5x7 pixel charset. You can view the characters
of the charset in Brick Viewer.

If automatic draw is enabled (default) the text is directly written to
the screen. Only pixels that have actually changed are updated on the screen,
the rest stays the same.

If automatic draw is disabled the text is written to an internal buffer and
the buffer is transferred to the display only after {func}`Draw Buffered Frame`
is called. This can be used to avoid flicker when drawing a complex frame in
multiple steps.

Automatic draw can be configured with the {func}`Set Display Configuration`
function.

The font conforms to code page 437.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.draw_buffered_frame
documentation:
Draws the currently buffered frame. Normally each call of {func}`Write Pixels` and
{func}`Write Line` draws directly onto the display. If you turn automatic draw off
({func}`Set Display Configuration`), the data is written in an internal buffer and
only transferred to the display by calling this function. This can be used to
avoid flicker when drawing a complex frame in multiple steps.

Set the `force complete redraw` to *true* to redraw the whole display
instead of only the changed parts. Normally it should not be necessary to set this to
*true*. It may only become necessary in case of stuck pixels because of errors.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.write_pixels
documentation:
Writes pixels to the specified window.

The pixels are written into the window line by line top to bottom
and each line is written from left to right.

If automatic draw is enabled (default) the pixels are directly written to
the screen. Only pixels that have actually changed are updated on the screen,
the rest stays the same.

If automatic draw is disabled the pixels are written to an internal buffer and
the buffer is transferred to the display only after {func}`Draw Buffered Frame`
is called. This can be used to avoid flicker when drawing a complex frame in
multiple steps.

Automatic draw can be configured with the {func}`Set Display Configuration`
function.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_oled_128x64_v2.BrickletOLED128x64V2.read_pixels
documentation:
Reads pixels from the specified window.

The pixels are read from the window line by line top to bottom
and each line is read from left to right.

If automatic draw is enabled (default) the pixels that are read are always the
same that are shown on the display.

If automatic draw is disabled the pixels are read from the internal buffer
(see {func}`Draw Buffered Frame`).

Automatic draw can be configured with the {func}`Set Display Configuration`
function.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.__init__
documentation:

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.write_line
documentation:
Writes text to a specific line with a specific position.
The text can have a maximum of 16 characters.

For example: (0, 5, "Hello") will write *Hello* in the middle of the
first line of the display.

The display uses a special charset that includes all ASCII characters except
backslash and tilde. The LCD charset also includes several other non-ASCII characters, see
the `charset specification <https://github.com/Tinkerforge/lcd-16x2-bricklet/raw/master/datasheets/standard_charset.pdf>`__
for details. The Unicode example above shows how to specify non-ASCII characters
and how to translate from Unicode to the LCD charset.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.clear_display
documentation: Deletes all characters from the display.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.backlight_on
documentation: Turns the backlight on.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.backlight_off
documentation: Turns the backlight off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.is_backlight_on
documentation: Returns *true* if the backlight is on and *false* otherwise.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.set_config
documentation:
Configures if the cursor (shown as "\_") should be visible and if it
should be blinking (shown as a blinking block). The cursor position
is one character behind the the last text written with
{func}`Write Line`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.get_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Config`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.is_button_pressed
documentation:
Returns *true* if the button is pressed.

If you want to react on button presses and releases it is recommended to use the
{cb}`Button Pressed` and {cb}`Button Released` callbacks.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.set_custom_character
documentation:
The LCD 16x2 Bricklet can store up to 8 custom characters. The characters
consist of 5x8 pixels and can be addressed with the index 0-7. To describe
the pixels, the first 5 bits of 8 bytes are used. For example, to make
a custom character "H", you should transfer the following:

- `character[0] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[1] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[2] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[3] = 0b00011111` (decimal value 31)
- `character[4] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[5] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[6] = 0b00010001` (decimal value 17)
- `character[7] = 0b00000000` (decimal value 0)

The characters can later be written with {func}`Write Line` by using the
characters with the byte representation 8 ("\\x08" or "\\u0008") to 15
("\\x0F" or "\\u000F").

You can play around with the custom characters in Brick Viewer since
version 2.0.1.

Custom characters are stored by the LCD in RAM, so they have to be set
after each startup.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.get_custom_character
documentation:
Returns the custom character for a given index, as set with
{func}`Set Custom Character`.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.1$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_lcd_16x2.BrickletLCD16x2.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.NFCRFID
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.request_tag_id
documentation:
To read or write a tag that is in proximity of the NFC/RFID Bricklet you
first have to call this function with the expected tag type as parameter.
It is no problem if you don't know the tag type. You can cycle through
the available tag types until the tag gives an answer to the request.

Currently the following tag types are supported:

- Mifare Classic
- NFC Forum Type 1
- NFC Forum Type 2

After you call {func}`Request Tag ID` the NFC/RFID Bricklet will try to read
the tag ID from the tag. After this process is done the state will change.
You can either register the {cb}`State Changed` callback or you can poll
{func}`Get State` to find out about the state change.

If the state changes to *RequestTagIDError* it means that either there was
no tag present or that the tag is of an incompatible type. If the state
changes to *RequestTagIDReady* it means that a compatible tag was found
and that the tag ID could be read out. You can now get the tag ID by
calling {func}`Get Tag ID`.

If two tags are in the proximity of the NFC/RFID Bricklet, this
function will cycle through the tags. To select a specific tag you have
to call {func}`Request Tag ID` until the correct tag id is found.

In case of any *Error* state the selection is lost and you have to
start again by calling {func}`Request Tag ID`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.get_tag_id
documentation:
Returns the tag type, tag ID and the length of the tag ID
(4 or 7 bytes are possible length). This function can only be called if the
NFC/RFID is currently in one of the *Ready* states. The returned ID
is the ID that was saved through the last call of {func}`Request Tag ID`.

To get the tag ID of a tag the approach is as follows:

1. Call {func}`Request Tag ID`
2. Wait for state to change to *RequestTagIDReady* (see {func}`Get State` or
   {cb}`State Changed` callback)
3. Call {func}`Get Tag ID`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.get_state
documentation:
Returns the current state of the NFC/RFID Bricklet.

On startup the Bricklet will be in the *Initialization* state. The
initialization will only take about 20ms. After that it changes to *Idle*.

The functions of this Bricklet can be called in the *Idle* state and all of
the *Ready* and *Error* states.

Example: If you call {func}`Request Page`, the state will change to
*RequestPage* until the reading of the page is finished. Then it will change
to either *RequestPageReady* if it worked or to *RequestPageError* if it
didn't. If the request worked you can get the page by calling {func}`Get Page`.

The same approach is used analogously for the other API functions.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.authenticate_mifare_classic_page
documentation:
Mifare Classic tags use authentication. If you want to read from or write to
a Mifare Classic page you have to authenticate it beforehand.
Each page can be authenticated with two keys: A (`key_number` = 0) and B
(`key_number` = 1). A new Mifare Classic
tag that has not yet been written to can be accessed with key A
and the default key `[0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF]`.

The approach to read or write a Mifare Classic page is as follows:

1. Call {func}`Request Tag ID`
2. Wait for state to change to *RequestTagIDReady* (see {func}`Get State`
   or {cb}`State Changed` callback)
3. If looking for a specific tag then call {func}`Get Tag ID` and check if the
   expected tag was found, if it was not found go back to step 1
4. Call {func}`Authenticate Mifare Classic Page` with page and key for the page
5. Wait for state to change to *AuthenticatingMifareClassicPageReady* (see
   {func}`Get State` or {cb}`State Changed` callback)
6. Call {func}`Request Page` or {func}`Write Page` to read/write page

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.write_page
documentation:
Writes 16 bytes starting from the given page. How many pages are written
depends on the tag type. The page sizes are as follows:

- Mifare Classic page size: 16 byte (one page is written)
- NFC Forum Type 1 page size: 8 byte (two pages are written)
- NFC Forum Type 2 page size: 4 byte (four pages are written)

The general approach for writing to a tag is as follows:

1. Call {func}`Request Tag ID`
2. Wait for state to change to *RequestTagIDReady* (see {func}`Get State` or
   {cb}`State Changed` callback)
3. If looking for a specific tag then call {func}`Get Tag ID` and check if the
   expected tag was found, if it was not found got back to step 1
4. Call {func}`Write Page` with page number and data
5. Wait for state to change to *WritePageReady* (see {func}`Get State` or
   {cb}`State Changed` callback)

If you use a Mifare Classic tag you have to authenticate a page before you
can write to it. See {func}`Authenticate Mifare Classic Page`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.request_page
documentation:
Reads 16 bytes starting from the given page and stores them into a buffer.
The buffer can then be read out with {func}`Get Page`.
How many pages are read depends on the tag type. The page sizes are
as follows:

- Mifare Classic page size: 16 byte (one page is read)
- NFC Forum Type 1 page size: 8 byte (two pages are read)
- NFC Forum Type 2 page size: 4 byte (four pages are read)

The general approach for reading a tag is as follows:

1. Call {func}`Request Tag ID`
2. Wait for state to change to *RequestTagIDReady* (see {func}`Get State`
   or {cb}`State Changed` callback)
3. If looking for a specific tag then call {func}`Get Tag ID` and check if the
   expected tag was found, if it was not found got back to step 1
4. Call {func}`Request Page` with page number
5. Wait for state to change to *RequestPageReady* (see {func}`Get State`
   or {cb}`State Changed` callback)
6. Call {func}`Get Page` to retrieve the page from the buffer

If you use a Mifare Classic tag you have to authenticate a page before you
can read it. See {func}`Authenticate Mifare Classic Page`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.get_page
documentation:
Returns 16 bytes of data from an internal buffer. To fill the buffer
with specific pages you have to call {func}`Request Page` beforehand.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_nfc_rfid.BrickletNFCRFID.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

documentation: internal
Reads and writes NFC and RFID tags

documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
This file was automatically generated on 2024-02-27.      #
Python Bindings Version 2.1.31                            #

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.IndustrialDualRelay
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.set_value
documentation:
Sets the state of the relays, *true* means on and *false* means off.
For example: (true, false) turns relay 0 on and relay 1 off.

If you just want to set one of the relays and don't know the current state
of the other relay, you can get the state with {func}`Get Value` or you
can use {func}`Set Selected Value`.

All running monoflop timers will be aborted if this function is called.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.get_value
documentation: Returns the state of the relays, *true* means on and *false* means off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.set_monoflop
documentation:
The first parameter can be 0 or 1 (relay 0 or relay 1). The second parameter
is the desired state of the relay (*true* means on and *false* means off).
The third parameter indicates the time that the relay should hold
the state.

If this function is called with the parameters (1, true, 1500):
Relay 1 will turn on and in 1.5s it will turn off again.

A monoflop can be used as a failsafe mechanism. For example: Lets assume you
have a RS485 bus and a Industrial Dual Relay Bricklet connected to one of the
slave stacks. You can now call this function every second, with a time parameter
of two seconds. The relay will be on all the time. If now the RS485
connection is lost, the relay will turn off in at most two seconds.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.get_monoflop
documentation:
Returns (for the given relay) the current state and the time as set by
{func}`Set Monoflop` as well as the remaining time until the state flips.

If the timer is not running currently, the remaining time will be returned
as 0.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.set_selected_value
documentation:
Sets the state of the selected relay, *true* means on and *false*
means off.

A running monoflop timer for the selected relay will be aborted if this function
is called.

The other relay remains untouched.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.get_spitfp_error_count
documentation:
Returns the error count for the communication between Brick and Bricklet.

The errors are divided into

* ACK checksum errors,
* message checksum errors,
* framing errors and
* overflow errors.

The errors counts are for errors that occur on the Bricklet side. All
Bricks have a similar function that returns the errors on the Brick side.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.set_bootloader_mode
documentation:
Sets the bootloader mode and returns the status after the requested
mode change was instigated.

You can change from bootloader mode to firmware mode and vice versa. A change
from bootloader mode to firmware mode will only take place if the entry function,
device identifier and CRC are present and correct.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.get_bootloader_mode
documentation: Returns the current bootloader mode, see {func}`Set Bootloader Mode`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.set_write_firmware_pointer
documentation:
Sets the firmware pointer for {func}`Write Firmware`. The pointer has
to be increased by chunks of size 64. The data is written to flash
every 4 chunks (which equals to one page of size 256).

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.write_firmware
documentation:
Writes 64 Bytes of firmware at the position as written by
{func}`Set Write Firmware Pointer` before. The firmware is written
to flash every 4 chunks.

You can only write firmware in bootloader mode.

This function is used by Brick Viewer during flashing. It should not be
necessary to call it in a normal user program.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.set_status_led_config
documentation:
Sets the status LED configuration. By default the LED shows
communication traffic between Brick and Bricklet, it flickers once
for every 10 received data packets.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off or show a heartbeat.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is will show heartbeat by default.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.get_status_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Status LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.get_chip_temperature
documentation:
Returns the temperature as measured inside the microcontroller. The
value returned is not the ambient temperature!

The temperature is only proportional to the real temperature and it has bad
accuracy. Practically it is only useful as an indicator for
temperature changes.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.reset
documentation:
Calling this function will reset the Bricklet. All configurations
will be lost.

After a reset you have to create new device objects,
calling functions on the existing ones will result in
undefined behavior!

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.write_uid
documentation:
Writes a new UID into flash. If you want to set a new UID
you have to decode the Base58 encoded UID string into an
integer first.

We recommend that you use Brick Viewer to change the UID.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.read_uid
documentation:
Returns the current UID as an integer. Encode as
Base58 to get the usual string version.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.get_identity
documentation:
Returns the UID, the UID where the Bricklet is connected to,
the position, the hardware and firmware version as well as the
device identifier.

The position can be 'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e', 'f', 'g' or 'h' (Bricklet Port).
A Bricklet connected to an {ref}`Isolator Bricklet <isolator_bricklet>` is always at
position 'z'.

The device identifier numbers can be found {ref}`here <device_identifier>`.
{{ device_identifier_constant }}

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_industrial_dual_relay.BrickletIndustrialDualRelay.register_callback
documentation: Registers the given *function* with the given *callback_id*.

variables.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.GPSV2
documentation: for backward compatibility

classes.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.__init__
documentation:
Creates an object with the unique device ID *uid* and adds it to
the IP Connection *ipcon*.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_coordinates
documentation:
Returns the GPS coordinates. Latitude and longitude are given in the
`DD.dddddd°` format, the value 57123468 means 57.123468°.
The parameter `ns` and `ew` are the cardinal directions for
latitude and longitude. Possible values for `ns` and `ew` are 'N', 'S', 'E'
and 'W' (north, south, east and west).

This data is only valid if there is currently a fix as indicated by
{func}`Get Status`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_status
documentation:
Returns if a fix is currently available as well as the number of
satellites that are in view.

There is also a {ref}`green LED <gps_v2_bricklet_fix_led>` on the Bricklet that
indicates the fix status.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_altitude
documentation:
Returns the current altitude and corresponding geoidal separation.

This data is only valid if there is currently a fix as indicated by
{func}`Get Status`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_motion
documentation:
Returns the current course and speed. A course of 0° means the Bricklet is
traveling north bound and 90° means it is traveling east bound.

Please note that this only returns useful values if an actual movement
is present.

This data is only valid if there is currently a fix as indicated by
{func}`Get Status`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_date_time
documentation:
Returns the current date and time. The date is
given in the format `ddmmyy` and the time is given
in the format `hhmmss.sss`. For example, 140713 means
14.07.13 as date and 195923568 means 19:59:23.568 as time.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.restart
documentation:
Restarts the GPS Bricklet, the following restart types are available:

```
.. csv-table::
 :header: "Value", "Description"
 :widths: 10, 100

 "0", "Hot start (use all available data in the NV store)"
 "1", "Warm start (don't use ephemeris at restart)"
 "2", "Cold start (don't use time, position, almanacs and ephemeris at restart)"
 "3", "Factory reset (clear all system/user configurations at restart)"
```

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_satellite_system_status_low_level
documentation:
Returns the

* satellite numbers list (up to 12 items)
* fix value,
* PDOP value,
* HDOP value and
* VDOP value

for a given satellite system. Currently GPS and GLONASS are supported, Galileo
is not yet supported.

The GPS and GLONASS satellites have unique numbers and the satellite list gives
the numbers of the satellites that are currently utilized. The number 0 is not
a valid satellite number and can be ignored in the list.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_satellite_status
documentation:
Returns the current elevation, azimuth and SNR
for a given satellite and satellite system.

The satellite number here always goes from 1 to 32. For GLONASS it corresponds to
the satellites 65-96.

Galileo is not yet supported.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.set_fix_led_config
documentation:
Sets the fix LED configuration. By default the LED shows if
the Bricklet got a GPS fix yet. If a fix is established the LED turns on.
If there is no fix then the LED is turned off.

You can also turn the LED permanently on/off, show a heartbeat or let it blink
in sync with the PPS (pulse per second) output of the GPS module.

If the Bricklet is in bootloader mode, the LED is off.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_fix_led_config
documentation: Returns the configuration as set by {func}`Set Fix LED Config`

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.set_coordinates_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Coordinates` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Coordinates` callback is only triggered if the coordinates changed
since the last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_coordinates_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Coordinates Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.set_status_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Status` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Status` callback is only triggered if the status changed since the
last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_status_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Status Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.set_altitude_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Altitude` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Altitude` callback is only triggered if the altitude changed since the
last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_altitude_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Altitude Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.set_motion_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Motion` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Motion` callback is only triggered if the motion changed since the
last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_motion_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Motion Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.set_date_time_callback_period
documentation:
Sets the period with which the {cb}`Date Time` callback is triggered
periodically. A value of 0 turns the callback off.

The {cb}`Date Time` callback is only triggered if the date or time changed
since the last triggering.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_date_time_callback_period
documentation: Returns the period as set by {func}`Set Date Time Callback Period`.

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.set_sbas_config
documentation:
If [SBAS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNSS_augmentation#Satellite-based_augmentation_system) is enabled,
the position accuracy increases (if SBAS satellites are in view),
but the update rate is limited to 5Hz. With SBAS disabled the update rate is increased to 10Hz.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

methods.name: tinkerforge.bricklet_gps_v2.BrickletGPSV2.get_sbas_config
documentation:
Returns the SBAS configuration as set by {func}`Set SBAS Config`

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2$nbsp;(Plugin)
:::

documentation:
